name: Graphite Blue Metallic
notes: Rare extravagance: Graphitebluemetallic is a deep blue-grey paint finish that is stylish and defines the proportions of the mid-engine sports car exceptionally well. The light grey-blue has only been available as a Paint to Sample option for a short time on the 911 since 2017 and shortly thereafter for the 718 models. Inspired by the grey tones of graphite, Graphitebluemetallic is enhanced with rich blue undertones. Graphitebluemetallic is a muted, somber blue-grey that is darker than many other shades of blue. Depending on the incidence of light, a slightly greenish shimmer may appear.

name: Graphite Grey
notes: Porsche Graphite Grey was a non-metallic slate grey offered on the 2015 50th Anniversary Edition 911.

name: Graphite Grey Metallic
notes: Graphite Grey Metallic is a modern take on classic Slate Grey with the added pop of some subtle metallic. As with most Porsches, black seems to be the dominant interior choice, but we sure wish that more people would add a dark red interior to this classic shade.

name: Graphite Metallic
notes: Graphite Metallic is a darker shade of gray that draws the eye in indefinitely. You can get lost in the paint as it is so capturing and you will always find more reasons to appreciate it.

name: Green
notes: The starting signal: Green has a long tradition at Porsche. To this day, the sports car manufacturer offers dozens of different shades of green, ranging from reserved and muted to extroverted. Through its colors, Porsche creates a decisive contrast with the precisely developed vehicle technology. The color names also contribute to this, and they always have special and even mystical names – especially in the green range. The color Green, also known as ‘RS Green’, was developed in 2007 for the 911 GT3 RS (997.1) and was chosen as the communication color. Green is an extroverted and radiant color that evokes positive emotions and expressively underlines the sporty character of a sports car.

name: Greenyellow
notes: Iconic vitality: Yellow Green or Green Yellow? The fresh green shade Greenyellow stands for joie de vivre and can also be perceived as harsh at first glance due to its colour presence. The lime green colour was formerly called 'Yellowgreen' and is firmly associated with Porsche history and with the 911 RSR, which caused a sensation at the International Race of Champions (IROC) in 1974. Greenyellow is a mixture of green and yellow that is often associated with nature, freshness and spring. Depending on the lighting and the viewer, the paint may appear to have more green or more yellow. The best twelve drivers in the world from Formula 1, NASCAR, Indy 500, Can-Am, Stock Car and Le Mans were set to compete against each other in the first season of the IROC in brightly painted Porsche 911 RS 3.0 models. These would prove to be eye-catchers not only due to the emergence of the colour television. The Carrera RSR #10 Petty in Greenyellow is one of only 15 differently painted examples that Porsche built for the 1973 IROC.

name: Grey Black
notes: Grey Black is a non-metallic grey that is visually similar to Slate Grey with a touch less blue and a pinch more brown in it.

name: Grigio Campovolo
notes: Grigio Campovolo is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023.

name: Grigio Granito Metallic
notes: Grigio Granito Metallic is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023.

name: Grigio Telesto Metallic
notes: Grigio Telesto Metallic is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023.

name: Grigio Telesto Pearl
notes: One of the newer and beautiful versions of grey that Porsche has to offer, Grigio Telesto Pearl does not fail to impress. The cars look like they are moving by just sitting still they look so fast.

name: GT Silver
notes: A beautiful aggressive looking silver that looks like you are flying by even if its parked. It looks good on all models and from every angle always... no wonder it stuck around.

name: GT Silver Metallic
notes: One of Porsche's longest running paint codes, and it is obvious why. It's impossible to find a Porsche not looking beautiful in GT Silver Metallic and it shows off all the designs that Porsche offer on their vehicles.

name: GT/RS Green
notes: A bright, flashy color for such a fitting beast, GT/RS Green matches perfectly with the GT3 RS. Without moving, it looks like its flying, and when its moving all you will be seeing are taillights.

name: GTS Red
notes: GTS red is a medium red that is similar overall to the Ferrari color Rosso Chiaro. As the name implies, it is most commonly found on the GTS sub-model of a particular Porsche.

name: Guards Red
notes: It's hard to know where to start with a color as iconic as Guards Red. It's one of the most popular Porsche colors of all time, offered in almost too many models and model years to count. In the 1980s, it seemed like 50% or more of the 911s, and 944s sold in North America were Guards Red. Porsches on the big screen reflected this reality, from the 911 cab in "Against All Odds" to the 944 in "Sixteen Candles." Guards red is bright shade that gets its pop from the fact that it isn't really a pure red (there's more than a hint of orange in it much like the earlier colors of Strawberry Red and Signal Red). Every Porsche that we can think of looks great in Guards Red. And that, in a nutshell likely accounts for its extreme popularity.

name: Gulf Blue (Dark)
notes: Gulf Blue (Dark) is the earliest Porsche color to carry the Gulf Blue name and predates the iconic light pastel blue associated with Gulf Oil–John Wyer racing cars. This version is a deeper, non-metallic medium blue with a richer and more saturated tone. While it shares a name with the later racing color, Gulf Blue (Dark) stands apart as a more understated and period-correct shade that reflects Porsche’s early color palette before the famous blue-and-orange livery emerged.

name: Gulf Blue (Light)
notes: Cult status: If a Porsche color symbolizes the sporty attributes of Porsche, then it is the combination of Gulfblue with the racing car paintwork from the 1970s. The specific shade of blue can be traced back to the light blue and orange Gulf Oil paintwork of the Porsche 917, which the British motorsport engineer John Wyer iconically staged in the Steve McQueen film 'Le Mans'. The name of the paint hails back to the company name 'Gulf Oil' itself, which acted as a sponsor for the racing team John Wyer Automotive. Gulfblue is a light pastel blue that is also called 'baby blue' because of its light and airy appearance. Combined with orange accents or stripes, Gulfblue creates a striking visual contrast that conveys a sense of speed and movement. Gulfblue was only officially available for a very short time in the mid-1970s and has enjoyed great popularity since its inclusion in the Paint to Sample program. In addition to the association with Gulf Oil's racing livery, another well-known example of a Gulfblue Porsche is the 1974 IROC 911 Carrera RSR #9, driven by legendary racing driver Bobby Allison.

name: Gulf Orange
notes: A smooth tasty orange, Gulf Orange is refreshing and looks great on all models. When the lighting hits it just right, oh how sweet it is.

name: Hellbronze Metallic
notes: Check the Rennbow master color chart and see if you can count how many variations on the champagne metallic theme Porsche has done since the early 1980s, we lost count at a gazillion.

name: Hellelfenbein
notes: Hellelfenbein is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023. An absolute Porsche classic — Hellelfenbein is more than just “white.” It’s a warm, creamy ivory with a whisper of yellow, the kind of color that makes early 911s and 356s look sculpted rather than painted. It has that old-world tone — soft, organic, and timeless — like polished bone or aged parchment. In direct sunlight, it brightens to a clean, buttery off-white; in shade, it deepens slightly, revealing its warmth and subtle vintage character. It doesn’t shout; it glows. The hue flatters the shape of any classic Porsche, accentuating curves without glare or harshness.

name: Heron Grey
notes: Heron Grey is more of a cool off-white than a grey. Like most Porsche greys, it's killer with a red interior.

name: Horizon Blue Metallic
notes: Horizon Blue Metallic is a soft silver-blue that reflects Porsche’s 1990s interpretation of a classic color theme dating back to the 1960s. The shade blends light blue with grey undertones, creating a refined metallic finish that shifts subtly with changing light. Calm and understated, Horizon Blue Metallic emphasizes the form and surface details of the 911 rather than relying on strong contrast.

name: Ice (Silver) Green Metallic
notes: Porsche Ice Green Metallic is a classic light metallic green with significant silver/grey, teal, or blue undertones, introduced in 1974 and popular through the mid-70s. It offers a sophisticated, shimmering look that shifts in different lighting and frequently described as an icy or muted green that highlights the car's body lines with a silvery finish.

name: Ice Blue Metallic
notes: Ice Blue Metallic is a classic silver-blue metallic that balances cool blue tones with a soft, silvery base. Often compared to the earlier Horizon Blue, it presents a slightly cleaner and brighter appearance, with metallic flake that adds depth under changing light. The color’s restrained elegance allows Porsche’s body lines to remain the focus while giving the surface a crisp, refined finish.

name: Ice Grey Metallic
notes: Ice Grey Metallic... exactly what it says. You can see the metallic grey everywhere but you get a white shade of the grey which makes it unique. It almost color changes from white to grey in different lighting and the paint glistens as it glides down the road.

name: Impulse Red Metallic
notes: A beautiful smooth dark and bright red that looks great in any condition. Rain or shine, dark or light, Impulse Red will look clean and stand out.

name: Inari Silver
notes: It's puzzling that this color is cataloged as silver, it's more of a light metallic green. Regardless, it's attractive and most commonly seen (to the extent that it's seen at all), as part of a smart two-tone scheme available on the 924 and 924 Turbo, paired with Onyx Green below the belt-line. It was likely named for the crystalline Finnish lake of the same name, rather than the Shinto god of rice cultivation.

name: Indiana Red Metallic
notes: Porsche Indiana Red is actually an Audi color, so it makes perfect sense that it was used on the 924 which was assembled at Audi's Ingolstadt plant. While rare as a Porsche color, it was a common Audi 4000/5000 color. An undeniably attractive red, it also found its way onto a few 911s via Porsche's Paint-to-Sample program.

name: Ipanema Blue Metallic
notes: Brazilian vibes: Ipanemabluemetallic is a turquoise metallic shade inspired by the colorful and lively atmosphere of the Brazilian district of Ipanema in Rio de Janeiro, which also includes the two-kilometer-long sandy beach of the same name. The attractive blue reflects the unique South American attitude to life and was offered as a special color only for the 2012 model year of the 911 (997.2) – at that time under the color code F2. Ipanemabluemetallic is often described as a tropical or oceanic color. Basically, Ipanemabluemetallic is a versatile shade that can be used as an accent color to add a sense of energy and excitement. The vibrant metallic effect of Ipanemabluemetallic ensures a cheerful and extroverted impression.

name: Iris Blue Metallic
notes: Iris Blue Metallic is a refined silver-blue that captures the understated elegance of classic European sports car colors. Its soft metallic character evokes shades like Opalescent Blue and Healey Blue seen on 1960s Jaguar and Austin-Healey models, blending cool blue tones with a subtle gray undertone. On Porsche models, Iris Blue Metallic presents a timeless and sophisticated appearance, shifting gently between blue and silver depending on lighting conditions.

name: Iris Blue Metallic (Pearlcolor)
notes: Delicate playfulness: Irisbluemetallic is a striking shade of blue that was available in various color variants at Porsche starting from the mid-1980s onwards. Launched on the 911 and 928 models under the color code 33P, ' Irisbluemetallic Pearlcolor' could be ordered starting from the mid-1990s for the 968 and 911 under the color codes 39N and 39V. The metallic shade is inspired by the color of the iris blossom, which unfolds a lush deep blue color when it is in bloom. Irisbluemetallic is a medium to dark blue shade with a cool and calming undertone. In its effect Irisbluemetallic is associated with serenity and tranquility.

name: Irish Green
notes: Irish Green, a medium non-metallic green, is a color whose charms start with its name and go from there. It's delightful on any car. There's a reason why Porsche chose it as the color for the one millionth 911.

name: Ivory
notes: vory is one of those colors that carries Porsche’s early DNA. It’s warmer and gentler than pure white, with subtle yellow and beige undertones that give it a natural, almost organic warmth. Think aged parchment or polished bone — elegant, timeless, and quietly confident. In sunlight, it glows with a smooth, buttery tone that feels more handcrafted than synthetic. In shade, it settles into a calm, off-white with a faint golden cast that flatters every curve of a 356 or early 911. There’s nothing stark or sterile about it — just a sense of warmth and heritage. Ivory is Porsche’s understated nostalgia — the color of simpler, purer machines built for the joy of driving. It’s refined without being precious, classic without feeling dated — the perfect hue for someone who appreciates authenticity over flash.

name: Jade Green
notes: Like any good early 70s period color, Jade isn't for introverts. Lighter and more blue than Viper Green, it still makes a bold statement. As an aside, A.J. Foyt drove a 911 RSR in this color during the 1974 IROC season.

name: Jade Green Metallic
notes: Jade Green Metallic is a metallic dark green which has some gray tones to it. It really fits the 356's class well as the color looks elegant and fancy.

name: Jarama Beige
notes: Jarama Beige is a warm, golden-tan hue with a hint of caramel — rich enough to feel luxurious, yet understated enough to let the car’s lines do the talking. It has that classic European elegance that ages beautifully rather than dates. In sunlight, it glows with a soft metallic warmth; in the shade, it settles into a smooth, sandy beige with a hint of depth — like sunbaked stone. Jarama Beige isn’t trying to impress anyone. It’s confident, period-correct, and unmistakably Porsche.

name: Java Green Metallic
notes: Expressive: Java Green Metallic is a lush green tone inspired by the intense green of the coffee plantations on the Indonesian island of Java. Java Green Metallic is also described as deep forest green or hunter green. The original Audi color is a glossy green with a slight yellow shimmer and a hint of turquoise. Depending on the lighting conditions, Java Green Metallic appears intense or can appear slightly brighter or more muted. The refined color is unforgettable, cool and sophisticated.

name: Jet Black Metallic
notes: No explanation necessary here. It's a pure black with a hint a metal flake.

name: Jet Green Metallic
notes: Breathtaking: Jet Green Metallic is a Porsche color that was available as a standard color on the first and second generation of the Panamera (970), introduced in 2009. A short time later, the dark green color was also offered for the Cayenne. On the American market, Jet Green Metallic is listed as Jet Green Pearl under the color code J6. If you compare the color with Forest Green Metallic and Porsche Racing Green Metallic, Jet Green Metallic contains the most blue and also has the highest luminosity. Jet Green Metallic is an intense dark green. The color does not necessarily stand out, but it does attract attention due to its impressive metallic shimmer. Jet Green Metallic is often chosen as the Paint to Sample option due to its color intensity. In combination with gold, Jet Green Metallic can create a strong contrast.

name: Kalahari Beige Metallic
notes: A refined blend of gold, bronze, and sand — Kalahari Beige Metallic brings a touch of quiet luxury to Porsche’s otherwise performance-first palette. It’s richer and more dynamic than a flat beige, with a metallic depth that shifts subtly under light, moving from warm champagne to a muted desert gold. This is the color of understated sophistication — think Autobahn miles at dusk, not track days in the sun. It carries that early-’80s to early-’90s Porsche aura: confident, elegant, and effortlessly tasteful. On a 911, 944, or 928, Kalahari Beige Metallic adds a layer of refinement that feels timeless rather than trendy — a color for someone who prefers class over noise.

name: Kelly Green
notes: Kelly Green is interchangeable with Viper Green. It appears that Kelly Green was used in the US while Viper Green was used ROW. It was a popular color in the 1970's first introduced with the famous 911 RS. "Kelly Green" — is a classic, vivid mid‑green used on Porsche road cars in the 1960s–1970s. Key characteristics: Appearance: A bright, saturated green with a slight yellowish bias (warmer than British Racing Green). It reads as lively and sporty, high‑contrast against chrome and bright trim. In sunlight it appears vibrant; in shadow it deepens to a richer green. Finish (originally): Typically a non‑metallic/solid lacquer on period Porsches (modern restorations may use modern basecoat/clearcoat or metallic variants).

name: Kiln Red Metallic
notes: Kiln is a dark metallic red that is a bit coppery and more subdued than Garnet Red.

name: Lagoon Green Metallic
notes: Lagoon Green under most lights looks more blue than green. Think of it as a very light metallic turquoise.

name: Laguna Blue
notes: Laguna Blue is a bright, solid blue most commonly associated with the Porsche 914, and like many 914 colors, it originates from the Volkswagen color palette rather than Porsche’s own. The shade is nearly identical to the earlier 914 color Olympic Blue and shares visual similarities with the Porsche 911 color Glacier Blue. Named after Laguna Beach in Orange County, California, Laguna Blue reflects the era’s coastal, sunlit aesthetic with a clear and vibrant appearance.

name: Lapis Blue
notes: Lapis Blue is a deep, jewel-like blue named after the semi-precious stone prized for its rich and intense color. In low light it appears dark and refined, while direct sunlight reveals its saturated blue depth and subtle metallic character. The result is a color that feels both elegant and dramatic, with a strong presence that highlights Porsche’s sculpted surfaces.

name: Lava Grey Metallic
notes: A more common color perhaps on the Cayenne than a sports car, Lava Grey Metallic is simple and slick. It's slightly more brown than the popular color of Seal Grey that it replaced. It's a smart, if conservative choice on any Porsche.

name: Lava Orange
notes: For over a decade, until about 1978, Porsche consistently had at least one orange available, often several. After that, the color vanished and the great Porsche orange famine lasted for close to 20 years. Happily, the shade that Frank Sinatra often referred to as "the happiest color" is a part of the Porsche palette again, looking especially nice on the Boxster, which is possibly the happiest of Porsches.

name: LE Bumble Bee
notes: Sold alongside the limited edition dual color (ivory/orange) 914 known as the Creamsicle, this one was black over Sunflower Yellow, hence the non-official name "Bumble Bee."

name: LE Creamsicle
notes: This is a rare Porsche dual color scheme consisting of Light Ivory over a shade of red-orange known as Phoenix Red. It's a nickname rather than an official designation. Creamsicle refers to the American frozen dessert on a stick that is white with orange stripes.

name: Lemon (Canary) Yellow
notes: Lemon/Canary Yellow is a classic shade of pure yellow that roughly approximates the shade of the fruit rind of the same name. Colors like this went out of fashion in the 1980s, but collectors go nuts for them now. It was unusual as one of the few colors that crossed over between the 911 and 914 lines.

name: Lhasa Green Metallic
notes: Lhasa Green Metallic almost has a teal/blue/gray tone to it and is very uncommon to see. The 924 really stands out when painted in such a pretty color.

name: Lido Gold
notes: Think of Porsche Lido Gold as Ivory with a touch of yellowish-green mixed in. It's a truly unique color that looks more like something from the early 1950s than the late 1960s. Porsche Lido Gold is one of those near mythical colors that even the most seasoned Porsche spotters have probably never seen in the wild. Lido is the Italian word for "beach." Appropriately, it's the location for one of the shots in the gallery.

name: Light Blue Metallic
notes: Porsche Light Blue Metallic is a pale metallic blue characterized by its calm, restrained presence. The finish blends light blue with silvery undertones, allowing the color to change subtly depending on lighting conditions. Clean and elegant, it offers a period-correct look that feels both technical and refined.

name: Light Bronze Metallic
notes: Light Bronze Metallic is representative of a family of platinum colors that Porsche seems to have had in its palette consistently since about 1982. It's a tasteful, muted choice for non-extroverts.

name: Light Green
notes: Refreshing and youthful: Lightgreen is a bright and bold shade of green that is presumably inspired by the light green to yellowish colour of birch leaves. This shade of Lightgreen is also listed as 'Birchgreen' – because the birch is a symbol of spring, new beginnings and symbolises light. Lightgreen has a high saturation, which makes a distinctive impact. This energetic colour was available on the 911 models (G-model) in the mid-1970s under today's colour code. Lightgreen is a fresh and airy shade that can create a calming mood.

name: Light Green Metallic
notes: Porsche Light Green Metallic is rather generic-sounding name for a stunning color. It's got a strong hint of gold to it and looks particularly good on a 911. Sadly, it's a near mythical color because it had the misfortune of being added to the Porsche palette at a time when bright, unconventional colors were going out of style quickly.

name: Light Ivory
notes: Light Ivory was Porsche's standard white until the purer, Grand Prix white superseded it in the mid-1970s.

name: Light Oak Green Metallic
notes: Symbolic: Light Oak Green Metallic is a medium, pale green tone inspired by the green-brownish color of oak leaves and the brown oak tree trunk. Oak trees symbolise strength and power and, despite their might, radiate a certain calm. The leaves of the oak tree also contribute to this effect, because they look completely different from the leaves of other trees. Light Oak Green Metallic embodies all of these attributes with its warm and earthy appearance that evokes a feeling of stability, reliability and durability. The color is known as ‘Oak Green Metallic Pearl Effect’ at Audi and has been incorporated into the Porsche Paint to Sample program under a new name. Light Oak Green Metallic is a medium shade of green and should not be confused with Oak Green Metallic. While Oak Green Metallic appears golden-green, Light Oak Green Metallic has more yellow and brown undertones.

name: Light Yellow
notes: Light yellow is not as the name implies a pastel shade, it's a more like a toned-down lemon yellow. It was a fairly popular 911 2.7 RS color, sometimes paired oddly with Viper Green Carrera side script and matching wheels.

name: Lighter Green
notes: Concise, penetrating and extroverted: Lighter Green is a historic Porsche color that was known as Jade Green in the past before having been renamed over the years. Lighter Green has been available on the 911 (F model) since the 1970s. Like many colors from this era, Lighter Green is a bright, captivating shade that makes a bold statement. Lighter Green is a vibrant, almost turquoise colour with subtle blue undertones. The color is lighter and bluer than the well-known Viper Green and takes on a warmer, more intense tone compared to Mint Green. In the 1974 season, a 911 Carrera RSR in Lighter Green competed in the International Race of Champions (IROC).

name: Lime (Birch) Green
notes: Anyone who was a fan of the 1974 IROC season will remember this extraverted shade of green. It was featured on the #14 Porsche Carrera RSR which was a practice car during the one and only IROC season to utilize Porsches. Porsche Lime Green is one of the more desirable bright period colors referred to by fans as "Skittles" colors, after the candy. Birch Green is the same color as the same color as Light Green. It is an older name and has the same paint code.

name: Lime Gold Metallic
notes: Lime Gold Metallic is a perfect description for this color which oddly enough, is a dead-ringer for the 1967 Mustang/Shelby color of the same name.

name: Linden Green (Chartreuse)
notes: Playful statement: Lindengreen, also known by the name ‘chartreuse’ - is a light yellowish-green shade that is associated with the original 911 from 1967-1973. Lindengreen was available as a special colour in the late 1960s/early 1970s for the still young 911 Targa (colour code 6710) and the Coupé (colour code 6767). Lindengreen can vary slightly depending on the context, its colour spectrum includes light to medium green tones and has yellow undertones. The vibrant and intense colour is eye-catching and can evoke a feeling of freshness and liveliness. Nature provides even more associations: The colour quality of Lindengreen is reminiscent of leaves in spring and summer, creating a link with growth and renewal. Overall, Lindengreen is a dynamic and attention-grabbing colour that exudes a form of playfulness and youthfulness.

name: Linen Grey
notes: More of a beige than a grey, Linen Grey was generally overlooked by the red, silver, white and black-obsessed Porsche buyers of the late 1980s.

name: Linen Grey Metallic
notes: Linen Grey Metallic is a soft, warm silver that seems to shift tones depending on the lighting.

name: Linen Metallic
notes: Linen is yet another variation on the platinum theme. Since the early 1980s, Porsche has been loathe to be without a shade like this.

name: Liquid Metal Chrome Blue
notes: Liquid Metal Chrome Blue is a highly reflective silver paint with a subtle blue tint, giving it a liquid, almost mirror-like appearance. The finish shifts dramatically depending on lighting and surroundings, emphasizing body lines while creating striking reflections that make the color especially photogenic.

name: Liquid Metal Silver
notes: Liquid Metal Metallic, a specially designed silver for the 918, is truly one to see. When the sun hits the paint, the colors and body lines draw you in and all you can do is stare. When the sun goes down, the color shines aggressively just like the car it was made for.

name: Lizard Green
notes: Porsche Lizard Green is a cross between Lime Green and Viper Green, and if you're enough of an extravert to own a Porsche GT car, you might as well go The Full Monty and choose Porsche Lizard Green.

name: Lugano Blue
notes: Lugano Blue is a striking solid blue that delivers strong color impact in daylight and a calmer, richer appearance at night. Introduced alongside the 992-generation 911, the color is named after the city of Lugano in southern Switzerland, reflecting a blend of modern vibrancy and refined European character. It nods to classic bright Porsche blues while presenting a confident, contemporary identity that adapts well to changing light.

name: Luxor Beige Metallic
notes: Reminiscent of the 1987-88 color Nougat Metallic, this a a conservative shade of bronze that suits the broad-shouldered first-generation Panamera particularly well.

name: Macadamia Metallic
notes: After being absent for a while, the color brown returned to Porsche in 2006, and Macadamia Metallic is one of the more attractive browns that they've offered. It's a bright root beer-colored shade that looks particularly nice in a convertible with a brown top and a sand interior. The color takes its name from a particularly rich tree nut that is native to Hawaii.

name: Magenta
notes: Magenta is a color that fans have dubbed one of the "Skittles" shades, after the beloved brightly-colored candy. It's famous as one of the colors chosen for one of the identically prepared Carrera RSRs that competed in the first season of IROC. Indy Car ace Gordon Johncock drove it.

name: Mahogany Brown Metallic
notes: Mahogany Brown Metallic is perhaps one of the scarcest regular production 944 colors. It's by no means an offensive shade, it just had the misfortune to be offered at a time when browns were seen as even more unfashionable than usual, and at a time when Guards Red was perhaps at the zenith of its popularity.

name: Mahogany Metallic
notes: Mahogany is one of the more attractive Porsche browns, it's more of a dark cherry color as the name implies.

name: Malachite Green Metallic
notes: Soothing harmony: Malachite Green Metallic is a green-grey color that was offered as a special color on the 718 Boxster/Cayman (987) and the 911 (997) in the early 2000s. The modern color is based on the semi-precious stone malachite, which represents a rich and strong green color. The natural stone is often used as a gemstone and is known for its unique patterns of lighter and darker shades of green that are created during its formation process. Malachite Green Metallic is an intense tone that covers the color range of medium to dark green tones. The color is a sage-like green that appears lighter than the other popular green metallic finishes such as Forest Green Metallic or Jetset Green Metallic. The exact shade of Malachite Green Metallic can vary depending on the context and is often associated with nature, growth and prosperity.

name: Malaga Red
notes: Malaga is a dark non-metallic cherry red used primarily on the 914 and 924, but sometimes seen in 911s via the Paint-to-Sample program. It's named for a town in Spain. As an aside, Malaga was also the name of a popular BMW color from the same period. This color bears no relation to it.

name: Malvern Red
notes: Malvern Red was an often over-looked one year only color that is darker and more of a pure red (less orange) than the far more common Guards Red.

name: Mamba Green Metallic
notes: Mamba Green Metallic is one of the most striking and polarizing finishes in the modern Porsche palette. Introduced primarily alongside the Macan and Taycan, it is a high-energy, contemporary color designed for those who want their vehicle to stand out rather than blend in.

name: Manaus Blue
notes: Exotic dynamics: Manausblue is a deep shade of blue named after the Brazilian city of Manaus, which lies at the point where the Rio Negro and the Amazon converge. In nature, the color occurs can be see, for example, in the bright plumage of tropical birds. The powerful effect of Manausblue evokes a sense of adventure and the desire to travel to exotic destinations. Manausblue was developed at the request of a customer as part of the Paint to Sample Plus process and is available as a Paint to Sample option. The color is a very deep, rich blue that enhances its impact when paired with a light tone such as Crayon. As a strong and striking color, Manausblue spans the range of medium to dark blue tones. The eye-catching and playful color creates an intense statement of vibrancy.

name: Maraschino Red Metallic
notes: Maraschino is a dark, non-metallic cherry red. It's a profoundly handsome color that inexplicably, almost nobody seemed to order.

name: Marathon Blue Metallic
notes: Marathon Blue Metallic is a pale metallic blue defined by its subtle grey and silver undertones. Shared with Volkswagen models during the 914’s production era, it reflects the collaborative nature of the platform’s development. The finish shifts gently in changing light, maintaining a composed and period-correct aesthetic.

name: Marble Grey
notes: Marble Grey is a very light grey that looks similar to the earlier 356 color Heron Grey.

name: Marine Blue Metallic
notes: Marine Blue Metallic is a cool-toned metallic blue defined by its subtle grey cast and even saturation. The color maintains a consistent, composed look across varying light, with metallic flake adding quiet depth rather than dramatic contrast. Its understated nature places it among Porsche’s more refined classic-era blues.

name: Maritime Blue
notes: High-quality blue tradition: Maritimeblue is a strong, non-metallic Porsche shade of blue that is derived from the Latin word 'maritimus' and means 'belonging to the sea'. The colour was available for the 911, 944 and 968 in the early 1990s and is now an essential part of the Paint to Sample product range. Maritimeblue is a medium shade of blue with slightly cool undertones that conveys a fresh and invigorating feeling due to its association with the sea. The color was inspired by maritime life and embodies a bold and eye-catching appearance, making it a popular choice for high-end vehicles and luxury brands.

name: Maroon
notes: Maroon is consistent with the relatively conservative/somber color palette offered on the pre-A 356.

name: Mars Red
notes: A beautiful, fast looking shade that fits in with its name since you will be looking like you are flying away to mars driving. Mars Red looks fast parked, and looks elegant and aggressive at the same time.

name: Marsalla Metallic
notes: The color is a distinct metallic shade with a hex code of #c97eba, giving it a muted, purplish-red or "marsala" wine tone. Rennbow only has this one example of this 911 Turbo in Marsalla Metallic, but it was part of the Porsche color pallette in the 1980's. It was available on the Porsche 928, 924, 944 and the 911SC. It is worth noting that the name Marsala has reappeared in more recent Porsche history, though typically as an interior leather color rather than an exterior paint. For example, the 2023 Porsche Panamera offered a Marsala and Marsala/Cream interior.

name: Mauritius Blue
notes: Mauritius Blue is a rich, non-metallic blue that sits comfortably between darker navy tones and brighter classic blues. Comparable in character to Copenhagen Blue, it offers a smooth, even finish that emphasizes body lines without dramatic light shifts. The color takes its name from the Indian Ocean island of Mauritius, aligning with Porsche’s tradition of geographically inspired blue shades.

name: Medium Grey
notes: Porsche does not offer a specific "Medium Grey" color, but rather a number of medium grey shades with different undertones and finishes. These Paint to Sample (PTS) and standard options vary in warmth, metallic content, and history: Fashion Grey, Arctic Grey and Stone Grey.

name: Medium Ivory
notes: Porsche favored softer whites with more yellow in them like this shade of ivory. It wasn't until the 1970s that purer shades of white like Grand Prix white became popular, although Medium Ivory remained as an occasional Paint-to-Sample choice as was the case with the 930 pictured in the gallery.

name: Meissen Blue
notes: Delicate history: Meissenblue is a historic Porsche color that was offered as a standard color on the 356 A in the late 1950s. The color codes of the classic pastel shade are 5703 and Reutter 703. The special shade of blue is named after the German city of Meissen, which is famous for the fine porcelain produced there. The Meissen porcelain factory first developed the shade Meissenblue in the 18th century; the light blue color is particularly popular for coffee porcelain. Meissenblue is often also called 'Powderblue' or 'Delftblue' and is slightly greyer than Gulfblue. The light blue shade is a soft, muted hue with subtle grey undertones that convey delicate and calm qualities. Meissenblue is not as vibrant as other shades of blue, but it conveys a wistful vintage feeling and carries a certain restraint. As a light shade of blue, Meissenblue harmonizes with red tones or neutral colors such as white, cream or beige.

name: Meridian Metallic
notes: Meridian is yet another platinum silver shade, the type of which Porsche has seldom been without since the early 1980s.

name: Merlin Purple Metallic
notes: Merlin Purple Metallic is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023.

name: Metallic Blue
notes: Metallic Blue is a straightforward yet distinctive Porsche shade that pairs a medium blue base with subtle metallic depth. The color carries balanced saturation and a gentle silver influence, allowing it to shift slightly depending on lighting conditions. It is sometimes compared to Gemini Blue and Minerva Blue due to their similar tone and era, though each maintains its own character within Porsche’s classic palette.

name: Metallic Dark Green
notes: A beautiful dark green that reminds you of a lit up forest. The reflections that can be seen off of this color are stunning and it looks beautiful no matter where parked or driving.

name: Metallic Dark Red
notes: Not much of a description needed here. For a color that suits a long-hood 911 quite nicely, it's a mystery why more customers didn't order it.

name: Metallic Green
notes: Metallic Green is a rare and jewel-toned color from Porsche's early 1970s palette. This shade is a bright, clear, and semi-jewel green that is often described as being close to a pure emerald hue. Its distinct character comes from the metallic flake, which makes the color vibrant and luminous without sacrificing its depth. It is less muted than many later metallic greens, offering a striking, jewel-like sparkle that truly captures the exuberance and style of its era. This highly unique color is a favorite among collectors due to its rarity and stunning visual pop.

name: Metallic Red
notes: Surprising that this color isn't as common seeing how common the name sounds, but it is beautiful nonetheless. Metallic Red fits Porsche perfectly with it's style and class and can fit into any collection.

name: Meteor Grey Metallic
notes: Meteor grey is a variation on the classic gun metal grey/anthracite shade. It's a bit warmer and more bronze than the similar shade of Seal Grey.

name: Meteor Metallic
notes: Meteor Metallic sits very humbly and calm, ready for the time to explode. It packs a huge punch while looking so simple. You would never know what the beasts behind the color is capable of.

name: Mexico Beige
notes: Mexico Beige came from the Audi palette rather than Porsche, which explains why it was a popular Audi 4000 and 5000 color. It's generally similar to Bamboo Beige. Understated, earthy, and perfectly period-correct. Mexico Beige is the kind of color that defined the late-’70s Porsche palette — warm beige with a touch of olive and just enough depth to give the body shape real presence. It’s not about flash or reflection; it’s about character. In bright light, it takes on a sandy, almost khaki tone; in shadow, it deepens toward taupe.

name: Mexico Blue
notes: Tropical lightness: Mexicoblue is a distinctive and light Porsche shade of blue that is inspired by the bold colors of the American muscle cars of the 1960s and 1970s. The intense blue tone resembles the color of the Caribbean Sea and a light blue agave. In the color spectrum, it can be classified as somewhere between sky blue and turquoise. Mexicoblue symbolizes liveliness and is a light to medium blue. With just a few interruptions in between, it could be ordered for the 911 starting in 1974 under the color code 336. The non-metallic color enjoys a timeless look because it goes perfectly with any sports car. Mexicoblue is a vibrant and cheerful shade of blue. It has subtle greenish undertones, which gives it a uniquely exotic look. Mexicoblue has plenty of fans in the USA, and one well-known Porsche collector has joined their ranks – actor and comedian Jerry Seinfeld ordered and received the last air-cooled 911 Carrera 4 S (993), which was built in the color Mexicoblue on 31 March 1998 at the Zuffenhausen factory.

name: Miami Blue
notes: Charismatic and modern: Miamiblue is a bright and bold shade of blue that was first offered on the 911 in 2017. The light blue tone is reminiscent of the sparkling water of the ocean and the clear sky on a sunny day – just like it also appears on the east coast of the USA. In 2018, Miamiblue was available on the 718 Boxster/Cayman models and a year later on the Macan. In the current Paint to Sample program, Miamiblue is available for the Panamera and Macan. Miamiblue is a bold shade of light blue with a slight greenish tint that conveys a cool, refreshing feeling. Miamiblue is often associated with tropical settings as it is a popular color for beachwear and resort wear. Miamiblue is vivid and playful, giving it an energetic aura that evokes happiness, relaxation and joy.

name: Midnight Blue Metallic
notes: Midnight Blue Metallic is a deep, dark indigo-blue with a rich metallic finish that reveals subtle complexity in changing light. In shade it can appear nearly black, while direct sunlight brings forward its underlying blue tones. The result is a sophisticated and understated color that maintains strong presence without relying on brightness.

name: Minerva Blue Metallic
notes: Minerva Blue Metallic is a high-impact metallic blue that blends strong saturation with subtle green undertones. In direct light the metallic flake enhances its vivid appearance, while softer lighting reveals a deeper and more composed tone. Named for the Roman goddess Minerva, the color stands as one of Porsche’s more expressive classic blues.

name: Mint Green
notes: Refreshing: Mint Green is a light and cool shade of green, which is derived from the English name of the mint plant. The color is associated with nature and spring due to its refreshing look, as it resembles the soft green tones of fresh leaves and freshly grown grass. The color has been available for the 928, 968, 911 and 964 since the early 1990s. Jade Green, which emerged in the 1970s, is similar to Mint Green, but the latter green is much lighter. Mint Green has been available in the Paint to Sample program since 2021. Mint Green is a pale green shade with a subtle bluish tint, giving it a pastel-like appearance. Overall, Mint Greenn is a soft and delicate color that conveys feelings of calm, serenity and relaxation.

name: Mint Green (Dark)
notes: Despite sharing its name with the lighter pastel hue of the early 1990s, Mint Green (Dark) is a distinctly deeper and more natural shade. Rich and organic, it reflects the true tone of mint leaves rather than the playful pastels of earlier eras—a rare and sophisticated take on green within Porsche’s palette.

name: Mirage Metallic
notes: Another variation on the metallic gold theme, this soft metallic is particularly uncommon special order 993 color.

name: Mocha Brown
notes: Mocha Brown is also known as Mocha Black (which is the literal translation of the German name for the color) In any event, Mocha is rather clearly brown, and not that terribly dark of one at that. It's likely the only Porsche color to also appear on a Starbucks menu.

name: Monaco Blue
notes: Porsche Monaco Blue is a light solid blue with gentle saturation and a refined, pastel quality. Offered during the later years of 924 production, it remains one of the less frequently seen colors from that era. The shade presents a calm, balanced appearance that complements the clean lines of Porsche’s early water-cooled designs.

name: Montego Black Metallic
notes: Montego Black Metallic is a fairly standard black with a hint of metallic. It's an early 944 color that can be mistaken for a solid black in low light.

name: Montego Blue Metallic
notes: Montego Blue Metallic is a rich, deep blue that shifts noticeably with changing light, appearing darker and more reserved in shade and brighter in direct sunlight. The color is named after Montego Bay in Jamaica, reflecting a coastal inspiration consistent with Porsche’s tradition of geographically influenced blues. Montego Blue Metallic has also appeared in BMW’s palette, though each brand’s interpretation maintains its own distinct tone and finish.

name: Moongem
notes: Rare and mystical: Moongem is a unique reinterpretation of the historic Porsche color Lilac from the late 1970s, which can only be found on a few models of 924, 928 and 911. The name and color are based on the gemstone of the same name, which is characterised by a milky, iridescent white-grey color and has a violet adularescence effect. This shimmering light effect is caused by the reflection and scattering of light and is created in the inner structure of the stone. The purple undertone in Moongem appear mild and creates a beautiful, iridescent dynamic when exposed to sunlight.

name: Moonlight Blue Metallic
notes: Romantic mood: Moonlightbluemetallic is a Dark Blue with a unique shine in its name. The night sky has a very special atmosphere in the moonlight, which the color Moonlightbluemetallic reflects. The soft and elegant shade has a blackish tint that creates a relaxing and calm atmosphere. Moonlightbluemetallic is a soft, muted shade of Blue with a slightly greyish or silvery undertone. The cool color radiates serenity and, with its metallic sheen, resembles the color of the sky shortly before sunrise or after sunset.

name: Moonstone (Lilac)
notes: As we've said before, Porsche color names are either hopelessly abstract or dead literal. This one is both. Porsche called it either Moonstone, or Lilac, depending on the source you consult. Either way, it's an exceedingly rare color that most people will never see in person.

name: Morningdewgreenmetallic
notes: Sensual temptation: the pleasantly shiny tone of Morningdewgreenmetallic is a sensation. The bright colour is an eye-catcher in every respect, as refreshing and profound as the morning dew that has settled over a landscape. Originally developed by Audi, this Light Green tone now enriches the Porsche Paint to Sample offering. Depending on the light, Morningdewgreenmetallic appears even more intense and reveals the different shades of Silver, Green or Light Blue. Morningdewgreenmetallic signals renewal and invites moments of reflection. The Green tone appears sensual – like the start of spring.

name: Moss Green Metallic
notes: Moss Green Metallic is a refined, jewel-toned shade that balances green with a subtle hint of blue. Often mistaken for the more common Oak Green, it carries a cooler, deeper character that gives the color a distinctive richness and understated elegance.

name: Murano Green
notes: Murano Green is a rare non-metallic blue-green inspired by the Venetian island famous for its art glass. Its rich, luminous tone captures the same balance of color and clarity found in Murano’s craftsmanship. Despite its beauty, it remains one of Porsche’s most seldom-seen shades.

name: Nardo Grey
notes: Nardo Grey is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023.

name: Nashy Blue
notes: New and inspiring: Nashyblue is a medium blue that was created as a result of the exclusive collaboration between the sports car manufacturer and the American designer and influencer Sean Wotherspoon. An art car consisting of 4 colors: Nashyblue, Seanpeach, Lorettapurple and Ashleygreen. The story behind the origin of the name Nashyblue is a very personal one: the designer named the newly created color after his son Nash. In 2024, the modern color Nashyblue will be added to the Paint to Sample product range and can be ordered by all Porsche enthusiasts.

name: NATO Olive Green
notes: Practical, functional and robust: Natoolive is not only a unique color name, it is also inspired by the classic military colour NATO-olivegreen, which is used for military equipment. The North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO), based in Brussels, as well as the national military organizations, use certain textile colors that are assigned to a RAL color. Military green is also commonly referred to as 'NATO Olive' or 'NATO Green', which in the German Bundeswehr is also known as 'stonegrey-olive'. Natoolive is a dark, earthy green with subtle yellowish or brownish undertones. The rugged look alludes to outdoor activities and is popular when a classic look needs to be both stylish and functional.

name: Nautic Blue Metallic
notes: Nautic Blue Metallic is a dark, metallic blue most commonly associated with the Porsche 944 and 924S. The shade carries a deep, oceanic tone with subtle metallic depth that becomes more apparent in direct light. Produced for a relatively limited period, Nautic Blue Metallic remains one of the less frequently seen colors from Porsche’s late-1980s palette.

name: Nepal  Orange
notes: Nepal Orange replaced Signal Orange for the last two years of the 914 run. Any of the oranges offered look superb on the 914.

name: Neptune Blue
notes: Neptune Blue is a vibrant, non-metallic blue with a deep and saturated tone. The color carries a rich ocean-inspired character, appearing bold and expressive in daylight while maintaining strong presence in softer light. Offered primarily on the Taycan, Neptune Blue stands out within Porsche’s modern palette as a confident solid blue.

name: Night Blue Metallic
notes: Enchanting blue: Nightbluemetallic is a dark shade of blue which, as the name suggests, is associated with the color of the night sky. Nightbluemetallic is sometimes confused with Darknightbluemetallic (color code Z5D), but is lighter and less reminiscent of cobalt in its color brilliance. The rich metallic paint embodies a sophisticated color that has also been available since the early 1990s as Nightblue Pearlcolour on the 968, 964 and the 911 (type 993, 996) with the color code 39C. The color should not be confused with the Porsche standard color Nightbluemetallic, which has the color code M5F. Nightbluemetallic is a deep blue that has a slight violet or indigo undertone depending on the lighting conditions. The bold blue color exudes something mysterious and creates a dramatic look with considerable elegance.

name: Nile Green Metallic
notes: Nile Green was an appealing and elegant shade that arrived at an unfortunate time—when green hues had fallen out of favor. Slightly bluer and less golden than Oak Green Metallic, it offered a cooler, more understated take on Porsche’s classic green palette.

name: Nimbus Grey Metallic
notes: Porsche's Nimbus Grey Metallic is a cool, mid-tone grey with a metallic finish that has slight blue or green undertones, reminiscent of a cloudy sky. It is a "Paint to Sample" color from 2023. It was originally an Audi color and is offered on Porsche models through the custom paint program. The name "nimbus" comes from the Latin word for dark cloud, highlighting its connection to stormy weather clouds.

name: Nogaro Blue Metallic
notes: Standing out from the crowd: Nogarobluemetallic is a light and bright shade of blue that is particularly effective on high-performance vehicles thanks to its sporty, dominant look. The color's name is derived from the location of a famous motorsport race track in south-western France – the Circuit Paul Armagnac, also known as the Circuit de Nogaro. The color was first used in 1994 when it was launched on what was then the fastest estate car in the world, the Audi RS 2 Avant. The vehicle was the beginning of the strong RS range, but its roots are in Zuffenhausen – the Audi RS 2 Avant was created as a collaboration project between Audi and Porsche. Nogarobluemetallic is a striking color that has a slight purple undertone and a distinctive metallic finish that is only visible under direct light. The shade of blue is dynamic and exciting and conveys a feeling of power and speed.

name: Nordic Gold Metallic
notes: Porsche Nordic Gold is a throwback to the shade of Metallic Gold offered in the early 1970s. It's roughly the color of your favorite Pilsner with a small touch of red to it. We think it's a particularly bold choice for a Cayenne GTS.

name: Nougat Brown Metallic
notes: Think of Nougat Brown Metallic as kind of a warm metallic caramel. It's a very rich color that looks better on a cabriolet than a coupe, particularly when paired with a brown top.

name: Nutmeg Brown Metallic
notes: Nutmeg Brown Metallic and the later shade Cognac Brown were among the last browns offered by Porsche until the shade returned to the Porsche palette with Autumn Brown Pearl in 1994, followed by Macadamia in 2006. Nutmeg is a particularly rich shade of reddish metallic brown that is pretty enough to give even ardent brown car haters pause to reconsider.

name: Oak Green Metallic
notes: The Turbo: Oakgreenmetallic is a classic Porsche colour that is a medium green metallic with a hint of golden grey. The warm green tone resembles the rich green leaves of an oak tree in spring and summer. The medium green metallic has been available until the mid-1990s for many Porsche models. Oakgreenmetallic is a deep and muted shade of green with a slightly yellowish or brownish undertone, giving it a natural and earthy look. The versatile colour can create a feeling of harmony, balance and relaxation. The 911 Turbo 3.0 (Type 930) became famous when it was introduced in Oakgreenmetallic. In 2024, Porsche released its own trainers based on this 1976 model designed by Dr. Ferry Porsche in collaboration with sports goods manufacturer Puma. The ‘Turbo 930’ trainers were designed in honor of Dr. Ferry Porsche's 1976 Turbo vehicle and are Oakgreenmetallic.

name: Oak Green Metallic Neo
notes: Porsche's Oak Green Metallic Neo is a modern, deeper, and more saturated version of the classic Oak Green Metallic, a heritage color that was a medium green with a gold-gray undertone. The "Neo" designation signifies its updated finish, which is a rich, modern interpretation of the original. It is a revival of a classic color, offering a fresh finish that respects tradition while feeling fully modern. Compared to the original Oak Green Metallic, the "Neo" version is a slightly deeper and more saturated shade of green. The original has more of a golden-grey metallic flake, while the "Neo" version is often described as having a flatter, more saturated look.

name: Ocean Blue
notes: Ocean Blue is a deep metallic blue defined by its rich saturation and subtle tonal shifts. In daylight it presents a confident dark blue, while lower light can reveal faint violet undertones. The metallic finish enhances its depth, giving the color a refined and dynamic presence.

name: Ocean Blue Metallic
notes: Inspiring: Oceanbluemetallic is a vibrant dark blue shade reminiscent of the color of the ocean on a clear day. As a historical color, Oceanbluemetallic is one of the first nine metallic colors of the Porsche Boxster (986), which were available at the launch of the new series in 1996. The color also became available on subsequent 911 models (993, 996) under the color code 3AY. Oceanbluemetallic is a deep and rich shade of blue with subtle greenish or blue-green undertones. The color's name also links it to the sea and the beach, allowing it to invoke an invigorating atmosphere that represents energy, vitality and adventure.

name: Ocean Jade Metallic
notes: Ocean Jade Metallic is one of Porsche’s more polarizing colors—best appreciated in person rather than through photos or online debate. A distinctive blue-green metallic, it’s often mistaken for the similar Wimbledon Green. When paired with a green interior, the effect can be bold, but the color itself carries a unique, modern vibrancy.

name: Olive
notes: Olive takes its cue from the green of Spanish Manzanilla olives, giving it a warm, natural tone with a subtle vintage appeal. Though it was offered for many years, few buyers selected it—making surviving examples a rare and distinctive sight today.

name: Olive Green
notes: Earthy green: the shade and its meaning are often already described in the colour name itself, as is the case with Olivegreen. The warm colour is based on the hue of the yellowish-green fruit of the olive tree, which gives it a natural and subtle appearance. In Latin, “oliva” refers to both the olive tree and the olive, its fruit. Olivegreen is an earthy shade of green with a slightly brownish or yellowish undertone that lends a charming appearance. This classic and timeless shade evokes associations with strength, resilience and a connection with nature.

name: Olive Green Metallic
notes: Olive Green Metallic, reminiscent of the earlier Granite Green, is a light metallic charcoal gray with a subtle green tint. This sophisticated blend of hues gives it depth and versatility, shifting gracefully between gray and olive under changing light while maintaining a refined, modern elegance.

name: Olive Neo
notes: The hero color chosen for the launch of the 911 Spirit 70 is suitably colorful – Olive Neo, a deep, richly saturated green inspired by the classic Olive and Olive Green Porsche colors, as well as the natural shade of the Tourmaline gemstone. To complete the look, subtle Bronzite accents can be found on the front and rear bumpers and on the car’s distinctive Sport Classic wheels.

name: Olympic Blue
notes: History in color: Almost every exterior color of the 914 model series shares one thing in common – they can also be found on vehicles from the parent company Volkswagen and vice versa. 914Olympblue is a pastel blue that is similar to the well-known Gulfblue and became known in the early 1970s under the name 'Olympic Blue'. The historic color was originally developed for the Volkswagen Beetle and was also available at the time for the Porsche 914 model built by Volkswagen. As a strong and vibrant shade of blue, 914Olympblue is reminiscent of sporting excellence and conveys a sense of strength, determination and performance. The bold, energetic color creates a strong and confident look due to its deep opacity.

name: Onyx Green Metallic
notes: Onyx Green is an exceptionally rare 924 color, most often found on 924 Turbo models as part of a two-tone combination with Inari Silver. It was seldom applied as a standalone color, making solid Onyx Green cars an unusual and distinctive sight.

name: Opal Metallic
notes: Nearly every year and model of Porsche post-1980 seems to have some variation on this color, a metallic platinum/gold. It's a low-key shade that suits the 928 rather nicely.

name: Orange
notes: Frank Sinatra once said that "orange is the happiest of colors." We'd have a hard time disagreeing with the Chairman of the Board. We'd be happy with it on any Porsche, but an orange 2.7 Carrera with a ducktail makes us positively week in the knees.

name: Orange (356)
notes: Orange was a popular color on this side of the Atlantic in the 1950s, but it didn't seem to catch on with the Germans until about ten years later. While not an uncommon 911/914 color, it's a rarely seen special order 356 color.

name: Orange Pearl
notes: Orange Pearl is a beautiful bright and absorbing orange that lies perfectly on the body lines of the 911. It stands out in a subtle way but all you can do is stare at it.

name: Orange Red
notes: Orange Red is exactly what it says, but the orange is the primary focus and the red tint lies on top leaving for a very pretty combination of colors to form this one.

name: Orient Red Metallic
notes: Orient is a particularly rich shade of red that has some of the softness of the 1980s Porsche color Velvet Red. It's a mystery why it wasn't more popular.

name: Oryx White Pearl
notes: Oryx White Pearl Effect is a pure white with a hint of pearl metallic. It's much more subtle than the metallic whites what were popular in the late 1990s, and contains none of the warmer overtones that those colors generally displayed. It was available via the Paint-to-Sample program.

name: Oslo Blue
notes: The original: The color Osloblue combines historical value with modern popularity. The dark, non-metallic shade is a blue that was available on the 356B in the early 1960s and was probably inspired by the Norwegian capital Oslo. The Scandinavian countries are characterized by a unique culture, impressive natural landscapes and a sense of community. Osloblue is a soft and restrained color with subtle greyish or greenish undertones that exude their calm look. The shade is used to create a peaceful and relaxing atmosphere. The simple elegance of Osloblue is often found on GT models to stylishly accentuate the sophisticated yet calming appearance. To mark the 20th anniversary of the sale of Porsche vehicles in China, the special edition 911 Turbo S 20 Years Porsche China (992) was released in 5 colors – one of which was Osloblue.

name: Ossi Blue
notes: Ossi Blue is a bright, classic Porsche blue known for its clean, non-metallic finish and lively character. Produced for a relatively short period, the color remains uncommon today, adding to its appeal among early Porsche enthusiasts. The name comes from the town of Ossi on the Mediterranean island of Sardinia, reflecting Porsche’s tradition of geographic color inspiration.

name: Oxley Green
notes: Oxley Green is a bright, saturated green with a subtle yellow undertone—warmer than traditional British Racing Green. Lively yet refined, it brings a playful energy to Porsche’s palette, striking a balance between boldness and sophistication while standing out as one of the marque’s more vibrant shades.

name: Pacific Blue Metallic
notes: Porsche Pacific Blue Metallic is a sophisticated blue-grey metallic that emphasizes subtlety over brightness. The finish highlights body contours softly, creating a calm and elegant appearance that changes with ambient light. It is often confused with Prussian Blue Metallic due to their similar muted character.

name: Pale Yellow
notes: A yellow that was hardly ever produced, Pale Yellow is a lot less vibrant than what you would expect from a yellow. Hence the "pale" part of the name, it takes yellow and adds a little less vibrancy to it.

name: Palladium Metallic
notes: Palladium Metallic is one of the more recent variations that Porsche has done on the Champagne metallic theme.

name: Palm Green
notes: Palm Green is essentially the pre-A 356 predecessor to the later, and much-loved, Irish Green. Deep and classic in tone, it set the foundation for one of Porsche’s most enduring color traditions.

name: Palma Green Metallic
notes: Palma Green Metallic is a vibrant, medium-bright metallic green with subtle yellow undertones. Lively and reflective, it highlights Porsche’s curves beautifully, offering a fresh, energetic alternative to darker greens like Oak or Olive.

name: Panther Black Metallic
notes: Porsche's Panther Black Metallic is a cool, jet black with a metallic finish that gives it a shimmering, luxurious look and adds depth. It features subtle blue or violet undertones, inspired by a black panther's fur, creating a sophisticated and modern appearance that is both striking and elegant. It pairs well with both dark colors like grey and silver, as well as brighter colors like red and yellow. This color, available through Porsche's 2023 Paint-to-Sample (PTS) program, adds a dynamic and refined dimension to any vehicle.

name: Panthero Metallic
notes: Panthero is a handsome, understated dark metallic grey that was perfectly in tune with the conservative early 1990s.

name: Papaya Metallic
notes: Papaya Metallic is of course named for the tropical fruit. It's one of the few true orange metallics that Porsche has offered. This color, along with Mamba Green are two of the boldest statements that you can make with a Macan.

name: Parliament Grey Metallic
notes: Porsche's Parliament Grey Metallic is a soft, elegant, and cool-toned grey with subtle blue or green undertones that give it a calm, dignified appearance. It is a "Paint to Sample" color, meaning it's a special-order hue, and was previously known as Westminster Grey Metallic. The metallic finish adds a sophisticated shimmer, and it can be seen on models like the 718, 911, Taycan, Macan, and Panamera. Parliament Grey Metallic was originally offered on Land Rover models in the 1980s and 90s.

name: Pasadena Yellow
notes: While officially cataloged as a yellow, it's really more of a beige. Pasadena was the last yellow Porsche offered until Summer Yellow was added in 1987.

name: Pascha Red
notes: Pascha was a dark cherry red consistent with the conservative and rather somber palette offered on pre-A 356s.

name: Pastel Beige
notes: Pastel Beige continues the legacy of the earlier Chiffon White. A creamy vanilla color that looks particularly sharp when paired with a brown interior and a stand alone class act.

name: Pastel Blue
notes: Despite its simple name, Porsche Pastel Blue carries more presence than expected, appearing richer and slightly deeper than a typical pastel shade. This non-metallic blue helped bridge the gap between softer early Porsche colors and the more saturated blues that followed, serving as an early precursor to shades like Mexico Blue and Miami Blue.

name: Pastel Orange
notes: A very popular color in the paint to sample catalog, Pastel Orange is desired by many. It is one of the brightest colors Porsche has to offer and looks beautiful on any model no matter any condition.

name: Pastel Yellow
notes: It's tough to add much to the color name here. This one falls squarely into the "literally descriptive" school of Porsche color names.

name: Pearl Grey
notes: Like most pre-A 356 shades, Pearl Grey is a pretty, albeit conservative color. It was consistent with the generally somber mood that prevailed in West Germany before the post-war economic miracle transformed the country.

name: Pearl White Metallic
notes: Pearl White Metallic is where precision meets elegance. It’s a multi-layered white with a subtle pearlescent effect that shifts between pure white, soft silver, and a faint golden warmth depending on the light. Unlike flat whites, this color has real depth — it doesn’t just reflect light, it plays with it. In direct sun, it sparkles with a clean brilliance, almost liquid in its sheen. Under overcast skies or at dusk, the pearl layer reveals a delicate iridescence — a touch of champagne or silver that gives the car a sculpted, premium presence.

name: Peridot Metallic
notes: Rebellious nature: The interesting-sounding shade Peridotmetallic has a golden-green colour and is inspired by the semi-precious stone peridot, which has a similar colour and a yellowish to light green sheen. At the LA Auto Show on 17 November 2010, the Cayman R was presented in the communication colour Peridotmetallic. The standard colour was then also available for all other model series until 2016. Peridotmetallic is a light green shade with golden or bronze undertones. The metallic finish gives the color depth and shine. As an accent color, Peridotmetallic exudes liveliness and energy.

name: Peru Red
notes: Peru is an unusual shade of non-metallic red that has a bit of terra cotta in it. "Brick red" is probably a more traditional description for this shade.

name: Petrol Blue Metallic
notes: Cool and elegant: Petrol Blue Metallic captures the intense Blue tones of the sea, its simple blend conveying both style and depth. The shade of Blue is a versatile color that combines well with other tones and represents the colors of the ocean. The color was originally released by Audi and is now available in the Porsche Paint to Sample program. Petrol Blue Metallic creates a glamorous look thanks to its metallic effect, without sacrificing a feeling of relaxed calm. The calming aura and confident character make Petrol Blue Metallic a rich tone that embodies a modern, cool aesthetic.

name: Pewter Metallic
notes: Pewter is another Porsche color from the literally descriptive school of color names. While quite handsome, it was outsold by the brighter Zermatt Silver.

name: Phoenix Red
notes: Phoenix Red is more accurately a blood orange color than an actual red. As with most 914 colors, it was not shared with the 911.

name: Pine Green Metallic
notes: A dark, refined green with subtle metallic depth, Pine Green Metallic embodies understated sophistication. Often overlooked due to its nearly black appearance in low light, it reveals a gorgeous shimmer and deep green character under closer inspection.

name: Pink Pig
notes: An interesting color scheme by Porsche, often used for cars on the track to grab attention. Very pretty and eye catching, you definitely won't miss it lapping you!

name: Platinum Metallic
notes: As literally descriptive as it gets for a Porsche color, it was added during the conservative backlash to the bright "Skittles" colors of the late 1960s-early 1970s.

name: Platinum Silver Metallic
notes: Platinum Silver Metallic is a classic, elegant metallic paint that sits between traditional silver and a warmer champagne tone. Silver is of course the national racing color of Germany, so as long as Porsche is around, there will always be a silver offered.

name: Polar Blue Metallic
notes: Polar Blue Metallic is an exceptionally light metallic blue that blends subtle grey and silver tones to create a cool, icy appearance. In bright sunlight the metallic finish comes alive, giving the color a clean, reflective character that highlights Porsche’s curves while maintaining an understated elegance.

name: Polar Silver
notes: Polar is a bright silver with just a hint of blue. Polar Silver Metallic is a historic Porsche color, originally offered on the 964, 993, and 996 generations of the 911, 986 generation of the Boxster, 928, and 968. Versus its period silver counterpart Arctic Silver Metallic, it takes on cooler and bluer undertones.

name: Polar Silver Metallic - 964
notes: Polar Silver Metallic 964 is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023. This color was originally offered for the Porsche 911 type 964.

name: Polar White
notes: Polar White is a crisp, bright white with a faint blue-gray undertone that gives it a frosty, almost glacial edge. It’s colder and sharper than Carrara or Grand Prix White, trading warmth for precision — the kind of tone that feels engineered rather than nostalgic. In bright light, it dazzles with a pure, icy brilliance — bright without veering into pearl or metallic shimmer. In shadow, that subtle blue cast emerges, giving it a sleek, technical quality that makes body lines look razor-defined. The finish feels cold to the touch — pure geometry, pure intent.

name: Polo Red
notes: Porsche Polo Red replaced Signal Red in 1966. It's a more pure, slightly less orange shade of red.

name: Polyantha Red
notes: Polyantha is a variety of rose, thus the name of this color is quite literally descriptive once you've decoded it.

name: Pongee Beige Metallic
notes: The 911 SC Weissach, produced only in 1980, was a limited edition design and only 400 units were constructed (Some sources states 406. In either case, a small amount were constructed and even fewer are known to exist today). All were sold to the United States. 200 were painted in Pongee Beige Metallic while the remaining 200 received a metallic charcoal gray color. Pongee Beige Metallic is a light, champagne-tinted beige with a fine metallic shimmer that shifts with the light. The base hue leans toward a pale taupe, infused with soft gold and a trace of silver, giving it a refined, almost silky glow — true to its namesake, pongee silk. In bright sun, it reflects as a warm, creamy beige with subtle golden highlights; in shade, it cools to a sophisticated greige with faint bronze undertones. There’s depth here — the metallic flake adds dimension without turning flashy, letting the paint read as both technical and natural.

name: Porsche Racing Green Metallic
notes: Modern interpretation: Porsche Racing Green Metallic is a deep shade of green and an adaptation of the classic British Racing Green color with a metallic finish added. Due to the historical color connection to racing vehicles, the shimmering effect creates a timeless appearance and emphasizes the classic vehicle character. From 2009 onwards, Porsche Racing Green Metallic was available for the two-door sports car models 911 (997) and 718 Boxster/Cayman (986, 987) for a few years. The color is also listed under 'Racing Green Metallic' with the color code 22G. Porsche Racing Green Metallic is a rich shade of green with a metallic finish that has a shimmering, reflective quality. This shade of green may vary slightly depending on the lighting conditions, but is usually a dark and intense hue with a subtle hint of blue undertones. Porsche Racing Green Metallic has an elegant appearance and suggests a refinement that merges tradition and innovation.

name: Prosecco
notes: An almost sandy, beige like shade, Prosecco is classy and fits the Cayenne perfectly.

name: Provence
notes: Provence: A beautifully evocative and sophisticated metallic lavender that captures the essence of its namesake, the lavender fields of the south of France. This color is a delightful combination of soft purple and subtle gray, often presenting as a light violet hue. It’s an elegant and unique shade that shifts beautifully in the light, offering a gentle metallic sparkle. Provence is a bold yet refined choice, standing out without being overly aggressive, and it imparts an immediate sense of chic, contemporary luxury to any Porsche.

name: Prussian Blue
notes: Prussian Blue takes its name from the historic pigment first developed in the early 18th century, a deep, intense blue long associated with fine art and technical drawing. On a Porsche, the color appears rich and sophisticated, offering a dark blue tone that carries depth in shade while revealing subtle vibrancy in sunlight.

name: Pure Blue
notes: Pure Blue is a vivid, high-saturation non-metallic blue developed specifically for the 2011 997 Speedster. Limited to just 356 examples, a nod to Porsche’s original 356 of the 1950s — the Speedster marked only the third production 911 Speedster, following the Carrera 3.2 and 964 versions. Offered alongside Carrara White as one of only two standard colors, Pure Blue became inseparably linked with this rare, heritage-inspired model.

name: Pure Green
notes: The freshness of nature: Puregreen is a bright, vibrant shade of green that mimics the color of fresh grass or the leaves of a green plant. It is a primary color that cannot be created by mixing other colors and is therefore often used as a base color. The invigorating atmosphere of Puregreen is associated with nature, growth and vitality. Puregreen is a clear, bright green with no blue or yellow undertones. It is used as a color tone to emphasise naturalness and create a refreshing environment.

name: Pure Orange
notes: Pure Orange is an intense and pure orange hue as its name recalls. It is a PTS color that was only offered on one 918 Spyder with the Weissach package. Because it was a custom order for Porsche's flagship hybrid supercar, it remains one of the most elusive colors in the brand's modern palette, making that specific 918 Spyder a significant "unicorn" for collectors.

name: Pure Red
notes: Porsche Pure Red was available only on the Titanium Edition Cayenne for model year 2006 only. It's often mistaken for Guards Red, but as the name implies, it's a pure red hue without the orange of Porsche Guards Red.

name: Pure White
notes: Pure White is Porsche minimalism distilled to its cleanest form. It’s a bright, neutral white with zero tint — no blue, no yellow, no warmth — just absolute clarity. The effect is clinical in the best way possible: crisp, technical, and brutally honest about the car’s shape and proportions. In sunlight, it’s brilliant and high-contrast, bringing out every crease and edge with razor definition. In shadow, it flattens slightly, giving the car a calm, almost architectural presence. There’s no shimmer or pearl — just a pure, solid finish that celebrates precision over flourish. Pure White is Porsche minimalism distilled to its cleanest form. It’s a bright, neutral white with zero tint — no blue, no yellow, no warmth — just absolute clarity. The effect is clinical in the best way possible: crisp, technical, and brutally honest about the car’s shape and proportions. In sunlight, it’s brilliant and high-contrast, bringing out every crease and edge with razor definition. In shadow, it flattens slightly, giving the car a calm, almost architectural presence. There’s no shimmer or pearl — just a pure, solid finish that celebrates precision over flourish.

name: Purple Sky Metallic
notes: Purple Sky Metallic is inspired by the fascinating hues of a rare violet sunset.

name: Purpurite Metallic
notes: A very nice mixture of dark blue and purple, the name comes from a purple mineral composed of manganese and phosphorus, some believe it to have healing properties.

name: Python Green
notes: Python Green is a bold, modern non-metallic shade that channels Porsche’s long tradition of vivid greens. Bright and unmistakable, it captures the energy of classic hues like Viper Green but with a sharper, more contemporary edge.

name: Python Green Chromaflair
notes: Few Porsche colors have a story as remarkable as Python Green Chromaflair. Its iridescent shimmer is created using the same substance found in the holograms on Euro notes, with each batch carefully tracked by the German government. The result is a breathtaking, color-shifting finish that’s as rare and exclusive as it is beautiful.

name: Quartz Grey Metallic
notes: Since the early 1980s, Porsche has had an addiction to smokey metallic grey like Quartz. This version has a touch of brown to it.

name: Quartzite Grey Metallic
notes: A very subtle grey, quartzite grey metallic lets the body line shine and flow flawlessly. It's not aggressive, but it could be. It doesn't stick out like a sore thumb, but it's an eye catcher. You can't go wrong.

name: Racing Green Metallic
notes: Not to be confused with the earlier 997 color of the same name, this version of Racing Green Metallic closely resembles Aston Martin Racing Green—the shade made famous by Aston’s international sports car victories, including the 1959 24 Hours of Le Mans win with Carroll Shelby and Roy Salvadori.

name: Racing Red
notes: Racing Red is a color offered in the updated Paint To Sample program for 2023.

name: Racing Yellow
notes: Racing Yellow replaced Speed Yellow in 2012. It's slightly greener than the previous Porsche yellow and opinions differ as to which one is truly the flagship Porsche yellow.

name: Radium Green
notes: Radium Green is a delicate silver-green finish that stood out among the few metallic paints of the 1950s. Soft and elegant, it captures the understated beauty of the early 356 era, offering a subtle metallic sheen that complements its vintage charm.

name: Rainforest (Jungle) Green Metallic
notes: Rainforest Green Metallic is an elegant, dark blue-green with a subtle metallic shimmer. Deep and refined, it’s a rarely seen color that rewards close inspection—its layered tones shift beautifully between forest green and deep teal under changing light.

name: Rally Yellow
notes: Porsche Rally Yellow was offered for just the first two years of 924 production in the late 1970s when bold primary colors were just starting to go out of style. This bright lemon yellow is almost never seen on North American Porsche 924s, a shame, the car looks spectacular in this shade.

name: Rallye Black
notes: Porsche paint color Rallye Black is a matte or non-metallic black with a unique association to Porsche racing history.
