The paint is a deep, solid black that offers a different aesthetic compared to Porsche's more common metallic black finishes. When clean, solid or matte black can appear even darker and richer than metallic versions. This finish emphasizes the lines and contours of the car's body without the distraction of a reflective gloss. However, any imperfections, such as dirt or swirl marks, are more noticeable on a flat black surface. This color is rare and highly sought after by collectors. The color draws inspiration from Porsche's rally racing heritage. Black decals were famously used on the bonnet, front wings, and doors of the 911 S models that competed in the 1971 East African Safari Rally. Rallye Black was a standard paint option for specific years and models, most notably the Porsche 911 from 2005 to 2011.

name: Raspberry Red Metallic
notes: There's no other way to describe it, Porsche Raspberry Red Metallic is a metallic pinkish-red. It's very similar to a color that Mercedes-Benz offered on the 190SL and 300SL several decades prior. Most Porsche fans have never seen Porsche Raspberry Red Metallic, but in person, it's quite striking.

name: Ravenna Green
notes: Ravenna Green is a bright, yellow-tinged green exclusive to the 914, originally sourced from Volkswagen’s color palette. Named after the picturesque Italian city, it perfectly captures the spirited personality of the 1970s Porsche era—vivid, playful, and unmistakably bold.

name: Red Brown Metallic
notes: A very uncommon color, red brown metallic shines bright and has a very unique mixture that you don't usually see. The result is a warm but stunning brown that looks brighter in the sun.

name: Red Metallic
notes: Yet another similar version to a Metallic Red in the Porsche catalog, this red stands strong and true. Red Metallic isn't afraid to be seen anywhere it goes and be proud.

name: Reseda Green Metallic
notes: Reseda Green is a light golden-green metallic offered only in the first year of 924 production and sourced from the Audi palette. Exceptionally rare, it bears a resemblance to the later Porsche shade Peridot Green. The name comes from a fragrant European plant often used in perfumes—or perhaps, fittingly, from the Los Angeles suburb that shares its name.

name: Rhodium Silver Metallic
notes: Rhodium Silver Metallic is one of the brighter and more stand out shades of silver you can find. It grabs your attention and takes it wherever the road car goes. Simple but special.

name: Ristretto Brown Metallic
notes: One of Porsche's newest colors, one of class which is fitting for the sleek design and usage of the Panamera. Can carry and travel a whole family, but also dominate a track.

name: Riviera Blue
notes: Charismatic: Rivierablue is a vibrant shade of light blue inspired by the brilliant blue waters of the Italian and French Riviera coasts. Rivierablue gives a cool, refreshing look and conveys an exciting feeling of speed. Rivierablue has been available on the 911 (993), 928 and 968 since the early 1990s. It was one of the market launch colors of the 911 (993), a number of press vehicles were also configured in this color and it can be found in a range of car magazines from this time. Rivierablue is a clear, light blue with subtle greenish undertones. The slightly metallic or pearlescent finish gives a subtle shimmer that playfully catches the light. Rivierablue is a popular choice for high-performance sports cars and is combined here with other bright colors such as red or yellow to create a striking effect.

name: Rock Green Metallic
notes: Rock Green Metallic is a deep, earthy shade that blends green with subtle hints of blue and gray. Elegant and grounded, it carries a natural sophistication—dark and understated at first glance, yet quietly striking in the right light.

name: Rolex Green
notes: Rolex Green is a rich, deep shade inspired by the signature color of the famed Swiss watchmaker. Luxurious and commanding, it radiates confidence and sophistication—its lustrous tone perfectly blending exclusivity, tradition, and timeless Porsche style.

name: Rose Red
notes: Porsche Rose Red is a bright red with a touch of pink in it. While purples, browns and pinks might in general, be polarizing Porsche colors, it's tough to deny that Rose Red is an attractive color on an early Porsche 911.

name: Rosewood Metallic (Palisander)
notes: Rosewood Metallic is one of the prettiest browns that Porsche has offered. It changes considerably depending on the light. In low light, it's quite grayish-purple, it direct sunlight, it appears quite bronze, and on a cloudy day, it's root beer.

name: Rosso Scuderia
notes: A very uncommon red from Porsche, this red is very bright and flashy in nature. Not many were made, and it is very rare to see one in person.

name: Royal Blue
notes: Royal Blue is a classic Porsche medium blue that sits comfortably between understated elegance and period vibrancy. Closely related in appearance to the darker early version of Gulf Blue — named for the Gulf of Mexico rather than the later racing livery — Royal Blue reflects the refined, restrained palette common to Porsche’s early years.

name: Royal Blue Metallic
notes: Adding metallic complexity to the traditional Royal Blue palette, Royal Blue Metallic presents a darker, more refined interpretation of the classic shade. Available mainly as a Paint to Sample offering around the 1998 model year, it stands out as a rare and subtle alternative to more commonly ordered Porsche blues.

name: Royal Purple (Lilac)
notes: Royal Purple is a non-metallic, medium purple. Fans of early 70's 911s often refer to bright period colors like this, Signal Yellow, Tangerine and Viper Green as "Skittles" colors, a reference to the brightly colored candy.

name: Rubin Red
notes: A beautiful darker, smooth, metallic type of red that is very fitting for showing off body lines.

name: Ruby Red
notes: Ruby Red and Signal Red are often confused for each other. Ruby is as the name implies, a purer red. Signal has a bit more orange to it, but the difference can be subtle in direct light.

name: Ruby Red Metallic
notes: Ruby Red recycled a name from the 356 era, but it's a completely different color, an attractive metallic with all the richness of the gemstone that it's named for.

name: Ruby Star Neo
notes: Ruby Star Neo is a new interpretation ("neo") of the very popular, historic Porsche color Ruby Star. Compared to the historic color Ruby Star Neo is slightly darker and less saturated with a modern touch.

name: Rubystar
notes: Cultivated determination: Rubystar is a striking and deeply saturated shade of pink, inspired by the colored gemstone of the same name. Ruby is known as a red gemstone, but the color variety ranges from pink to purple. Pink rubies have a high degree of saturation and can range from a pale, almost pink- orange hue to a bright, bold pink or a deep rose-red. Rubystar is a historic Porsche color from the early 1990s that has become an iconic color as the bright introductory color of the legendary 911 Carrera RS 3.6 (964). Although the striking hue is perceived as polarising, Rubystar is a popular color choice that appeals to women and men alike. The color is called Rubystar, formerly Rubystone, and was available under the color code 82N /M3B for the models 928, 944, 968 and 911 (964). Rubystar falls between the violet and red shades and is a multifaceted, vibrant and intense magenta color with a slight cold undertone. The unique and attractive color brilliance symbolises vitality and self-confidence. At the end of 2022, Porsche launched Rubystarneo, a modern interpretation of this color, which is available as a special color in the standard range.

name: Rubystone Red (Ruby Star)
notes: Porsche Rubystone Red (also referred to as Ruby Star), is a fairly polarizing color--Some people love it, some can't deal with it. But if you're down with the concept of a retina-searing pinkish-magenta 911, it's quite striking, and it's become popular again via Porsche's Paint-to-Sample program, and it the new color that it inspired, Ruby Star Neo. Welcome back, we say!

name: Sable Brown Metallic
notes: Sable Brown is closer to a smokey quartz grey, than a traditional brown. It's similar to the earlier shade of Copper Bronze Metallic. Browns in general were on their way out by the time this color entered the Porsche palette.

name: Sahara Beige
notes: Warm, sandy, and unmistakably vintage — Sahara Beige is pure ’70s Porsche character. It’s a mid-tone beige with a soft golden-yellow base, the color of sun-baked stone and desert heat. There’s a touch of warmth that keeps it from feeling flat — it’s grounded but glowing, a tone that feels naturally at home on both chrome-bumpered classics and minimalist early impact-bumper cars. In full sun, Sahara Beige opens up with a rich wheat-gold brightness; in shade, it mellows into a smooth, earthy tan with a hint of ochre. The result is a hue that’s calm but confident — more “continental tour” than “concours lawn.”

name: Salmon Metallic
notes: Salmon Metallic was another color whose name was actually quite descriptive. It's a coppery metallic orange that looks a great deal like the color of a fresh salmon filet.

name: San Marino Blue Metallic
notes: Breathtaking: Sanmarinobluemetallic is a modern tone of Blue that bears the name of the mountainous enclave in the north of central Italy. One of the oldest republics in the world, San Marino is a medieval old town surrounded by walls and known for its narrow, cobbled streets. The view from there of the Adriatic Sea covers the horizon in a stylish blue. Sanmarinobluemetallic is a deep, rich shade of Blue that is often used in high-performance vehicles. The color was developed at the request of a customer as part of the Paint to Sample Plus process and is available as a Paint to Sample option. Sanmarinobluemetallic is a Metallic Blue with subtle Violet undertones. The clear and rich Blue has a pearlescent metal finish that captures the light deeply. When you look at the color, associations with power, performance and luxury arise. In combination with other strong colours such as Red or Yellow, an eye-catching contrast can be achieved.

name: Sand Beige
notes: Sand Beige sits right between tan and taupe, with a smooth, natural undertone that feels like sunlit leather or fine sand along the Mediterranean coast. It’s not a loud color; it’s refined, inviting, and full of vintage charm. In bright light, it shows as a creamy beige with soft golden warmth; in shadow, it deepens into a muted caramel-tan, giving the bodywork a subtle, sculpted presence. There’s a reason this hue became a Porsche interior and exterior staple through the ’60s and ’70s — it complements every curve without distraction.

name: Sand Grey
notes: Sand Grey was typical of most pre-A 356 colors, conservative and somewhat somber.

name: Sand White
notes: Sand White is a laid back white that almost blends in with its surroundings. It stands out when you look closely at its beauty, but it is classy and laid back.

name: Sand Yellow
notes: Sand Yellow was an unusual color to see on an SUV, kudos to Porsche for offering it, and to the five or so people who actually ordered it. It looks a fair bit like the 1970s color Talbot Yellow.

name: Sapphire Blue Metallic
notes: A noble option: Sapphirebluemetallic is a deep, rich shade of blue that was inspired by the intense blue tones of the precious gemstone sapphire. The modern color unites the history of the wide range of Porsche blue tones. Sapphirebluemetallic is a stylish color that is inspired by 'Minervablue' of the 1970s and 1980s. From 2014, Sapphirebluemetallic has adorned all Porsche model series except the Cayenne for several years. Today the color is available as an option in the Paint to Sample program for the Porsche customers.

name: Sapphire Metallic
notes: Sapphire is a bit of a misnomer for this color. Rather than mimicking the deep blue of the gemstone, this is actually a metallic grey with a faint hint of blue in it.

name: Satin Black Metallic
notes: Satin Black Metallic is similar to the later shade of Basalt Black. It's a metallic black with ever so slight purple overtones. It was one of the colors offered on the 1988 Canadian Celebration Edition 944s.

name: Saturn Metallic
notes: A classic creamy brown, Saturn Metallic has hints of red under the right light and never fails to make the 924 stand out.

name: Saturn Yellow
notes: Saturn was one of several yellows Porsche offered on the 914. It was generally the brighter shade in years where two yellows were available.

name: Scarlet Red
notes: 914 Colors came from VW, Scarlet was essentially the VW equivalent of Guards Red, a bright non-metallic red with a hint of orange.

name: Schiefer
notes: A gray with a very very light blue tint, Schiefer is a beautiful classic blue that will look good any day.

name: Sea Foam Green Metallic
notes: Not to be confused with Sea Foam, the color Sea Foam Green Metallic is a masterclass in Porsche’s ability to blend vintage soul with modern precision. While many greens in the automotive world lean toward the aggressive or earthy, Sea Foam Green Metallic captures a rare sense of translucent serenity and sophistication while also subtle.

name: Sea Green
notes: Sea Green was a light, non-metallic gray-green offered during the final three years of the short-wheelbase 911 and 912. Subtle and serene, it’s a rare color that perfectly captures the understated charm of Porsche’s late-1960s palette.

name: Seal Grey Metallic
notes: Seal Grey might just be the Guards Red of the early aughts in terms of popularity. 996 Twin Turbos are particularly common in this tasteful, very low-key color which acted as superb law enforcement camouflage.

name: Sepia Brown
notes: If you can get past brown car phobia, Porsche Sepia Brown is a pleasant long-hood 911 color. It's a lighter brown with a hint of yellowish-orange to it. Unfortunately, a huge percentage of Sepia Porsche 911s fell victim to color changes in the 1980s-1990s.

name: Shade Green Metallic
notes: Shade Green Metallic is a pastel green that was first introduced with the 992 911 Dakar. Both the sportiness and the modern aspect of the color result in a unique visual character, enhancing the vehicle's dynamic allure.

name: Shark Blue
notes: Shark Blue is a bold, non-metallic color introduced with the 992.1 generation 911 GT3. Its intense, modern tone reflects Porsche’s continued move toward expressive, high-impact colors designed to stand out on both road and track. It nods back to earlier Porsche blues such as Arrow, Mexico, and Voodoo Blue, extending a long lineage of vibrant Porsche blues.

name: Sholar Blue
notes: Created for the PCA 70th Anniversary 911 Club Coupe, Sholar Blue pays tribute to PCA founder Bill Sholar. This elegant metallic finish draws inspiration from the deep, refined tones of Club Blau. Here, that signature color of the PCA 60th Anniversary Club Coupe is reimagined as a modern, shimmering evolution.

name: Shore Blue
notes: Introduced on the 2024 911 ST, Shore Blue captures the serene depth of ocean water and the clarity of open sky. This metallic finish brings a calm yet vibrant character, perfectly complementing the ST’s blend of heritage design and modern performance.

name: Sienna Metallic
notes: Sienna is a metallic red/orange that is often confused with the later shade Kiln Red.

name: Sienna Red Metallic
notes: A beautiful dark red metallic, Sienna Red Metallic looks good from all angles in all conditions.

name: Signal Green
notes: Strikingly different: Signalgreen is a bright and historic Porsche color, that resembles Vipergreen and is especially well-known on the Porsche Transaxle models. The origin of the color goes back to the end of the 1960s and has gone through five different color codes. Signalgreen is a clearly recognizable shade of green that has an important function as a visual signal and stands for starting or permission. The color has a calming and refreshing effect and is often associated with nature, balance and harmony.

name: Signal Orange
notes: Signal orange can be distinguished from other Porsche oranges by being a touch more yellow. Think of the color of a ripe pumpkin, and that's pretty much Signal Orange.

name: Signal Red
notes: Signal Red is a bright red with a touch of orange. It's similar to the later, über-popular shade, Guards Red.

name: Signal Yellow
notes: Signal Yellow is one of the much-desired, bright, period colors that enthusiasts playfully refer to as "Skittles" colors, a reference to the brightly-colored fruit-flavored candy. It's a yellow with more than a little red in it.

name: Silver
notes: A very basic color, but it works with any car. Silver has been around since the beginning of paint and the start of Porsche and has since taken on many forms.

name: Silver Metallic
notes: A iconic color for any German car.

name: Silver Rose
notes: Silver Rose is a smokey silver with a hint of raspberry to it. It was available only on the limited edition 944 Turbo S.

name: Skopos grey
notes: Skopos Grey is a rare Porsche paint color, likely with a metallic finish that gives it depth . The name "Skopos" may suggest a unique, perhaps purposeful or measured, grey hue. It is a dark grey with subtle undertones or a lighter, more reflective grey. As a non-standard color, Skopos Grey is offered as a Paint to Sample (PTS) option, which allows customers to choose a color that is not part of the standard Porsche palette. This further contributes to its rarity.

name: Sky Blue
notes: Despite its name, Sky Blue isn’t the pale pastel one might expect. Richer and slightly green-tinged, it presents a deeper, more sophisticated tone that flatters the curves of a 356. Closely related to the earlier Oslo Blue, Sky Blue appeared on late 356s and carried over to early 911s. It is sometimes mistakenly referred to as “Enamel Blue.”

name: Slate Blue Metallic
notes: Slate Blue Metallic is a refined, medium-dark metallic blue with subtle gray undertones, giving it a sophisticated, understated appearance. Its blend of cool blue and slate tones create a color that shifts beautifully with the light, making it equally at home on classic and modern Porsches alike.

name: Slate Grey
notes: Slate Grey is a particularly attractive non-metallic grey with a hint of blue to it. It's claim to fame? Slate was Steve McQueen's favorite color. The 911S seen at the beginning of the film "Le Mans" was painted Slate Grey, and his very early Turbo Carrera was painted Slate Grey to sample.

name: Slate Grey Metallic
notes: From the early 1980s to the present, Porsche has never been without a metallic dark grey. It's always been both a popular and conservative choice.

name: Slate Grey Neo
notes: Porsche Slate Grey Neo is a modern interpretation of the classic Slate Grey, appearing as a bold, pure, dark grey with no discernible undertones, offering a sophisticated and timeless look to Porsche vehicles. Introduced with the 2025 model year for cars like the 911 and Taycan, it replaces Arctic Grey and is one of Porsche's premium, "Legends" colors, blending heritage with innovation.

name: Smyrna Green
notes: Smyrnagreen is a light, non-metallic grey-green whose name is presumably derived from the historic Greek city of Smyrna and its cultural heritage. The colour is a historic Porsche series colour and was available from 1962 on the 356 B under the colour codes 6210 and R744. Smyrnagreen has a mint green undertone, but it is not as bright and the overall effect is perceived as subtle and unique.

name: South Sea Blue Metalic
notes: A bright and vibrant metallic shade of blue, presumably named after the color of the South Seas, which are known for their crystal- clear blue waters. The lively blue color with a slight silver and turquoise undertone gives it a tropical and exotic feel. Mercedes introduced South Sea Blue Metallic as part of its MANUFAKTUR / Designo palette in the 2010s and was adopted by Porsche through the Paint-to-Sample (PTS) program.

name: Speed Yellow
notes: Speed Yellow is like Guards Red in the sense that few Porsche sports cars look bad in it, but unlike red, it takes a special kind of owner to live with a bright yellow car. A little redder of a yellow than the later shade of Racing Yellow, it's visually similar to yellows used by other sports car makers--Ferrari's Giallo Fly, Lotus Yellow and Corvette Yellow are not dissimilar shades. Speed Yellow is far more common in the 911 and the Boxster/Cayman than it is in the 968 where it is exceedingly rare.

name: Speedster Blue
notes: Named for the legendary Porsche Speedster, Speedster Blue is a rich, non-metallic medium blue that balances sportiness and sophistication. It’s a timeless shade that reflects Porsche’s enduring mastery of blue hues.

name: Sports Classic Grey
notes: The shade chosen for one of the most desirable 997 variants, the RoW only Sports Classic was a throwback to Dolphin Grey, a color from the 356 era.

name: Steel Blue Metallic
notes: A very silvery shade of blue, Steel Blue Metallic was a sleek and sophisticated color choice for the 911. Its subtle metallic finish highlights the car’s curves and proportions beautifully, emphasizing Porsche’s design purity.

name: Stone Grey
notes: Porsche Stone Grey is actually less a grey and more a greenish-beige/khaki color. It's surprisingly attractive in person, particularly when paired with a red interior. Somewhat surprisingly, it's made a recent comeback via Porsche's Paint-to-Sample ("PTS") program.

name: Stone Grey Metallic
notes: Stone grey metallic is one of multiple variations on dark metallic grey that Porsche has consistently offered since the 1980s.

name: Strawberry Red
notes: Strawberry Red was one of the exceptions to the generally muted early 356 palette.

name: Summer Yellow (Early)
notes: Summer yellow (not to be confused with the very different 1987-88 color of the same name) is essentially the same color as Sunflower Yellow, renamed for the last two years of the 914 model run.

name: Summer Yellow (Late)
notes: Summer yellow is a pleasant, creamy light yellow that falls somewhere in between the later shade Pastel Yellow and the earlier shade Light Yellow in terms of intensity. Its two year-only status contributes to its scarcity.

name: Sunflower Yellow
notes: Sunflower Yellow was a pure shade of yellow that was like most 914 colors, VW-sourced. It somewhat resembled the Porsche shade of Talbot Yellow. It was inexplicably renamed "Summer Yellow" for 1975-76, the last two years of the 914 model run.

name: Superior Red Metallic
notes: Superior Red is a dark metallic burgundy that has one of the clunkier German translations. Extra points if you can pronounce it.

name: Surinam Red Metallic
notes: A laid back, dark red with lighter tones, Surinam Red always catches the eye on any Porsche model.

name: Tahoe Blue Metallic
notes: Named after the iconic California–Nevada lake, Tahoe Blue is a lighter, slightly more turquoise interpretation of Porsche’s earlier Minerva Blue. Its vivid yet refined tone captures a sense of depth and clarity reminiscent of its namesake waters.

name: Talbot Yellow
notes: Talbot Yellow was the last true yellow offered by Porsche until Summer Yellow was added in 1987 (Pasadena Yellow was more of a tan than a yellow). It's a richer, more buttery yellow than Signal or Light Yellow and tends to pair better with brown, cinnamon or cork interiors than black.

name: Tangerine
notes: Tangerine is one of the more popular "Skittles" colors from the 1960s and 1970s. It was the reddest of the oranges offered by Porsche during that time period, and was one of just several colors common to both the 911 and 914.

name: Teal Blue Metallic
notes: Teal Blue Metallic blends blue and green tones with a refined metallic shimmer, creating a rich color that shifts beautifully under changing light. Lively yet composed, it captures the dynamic energy of its namesake shade while maintaining a timeless elegance across Porsche’s design language.

name: Terra Cotta
notes: Terra Cotta was as the name implies a light brick orange/brown/red shade. It's a particularly uncommon pre-A 356 color.

name: Terra Grey Metallic
notes: Porsche's Terra Grey Metallic is a medium-toned, warm grey with a metallic finish, part of the 2023 Paint-to-Sample (PTS) program. It has a glossy appearance and is a rich, earthy shade that is more golden or bronze than other greys like Meteor Grey Metallic, which is typically a cooler, bluish grey. A warm, earthy grey with golden or bronze undertones, described as being warmer than Seal Grey and brighter than Agate Grey. Metallic, creating a high-gloss, high-precision finish that reflects light and adds depth to the color. The color can appear different depending on lighting and surrounding colors.

name: Tin (Zinc) Metallic
notes: Tin is a slightly smokey shade of silver with a hint of platinum in it. And oh, does it look nice with a red interior. As an aside, in English, the color is sometimes referred to as Zinc, however, the German name for the color, "zinn" translates to "tin" in English. Porsche Zinc Metallic refers to an older Porsche paint color — a metallic silver-gray tone that was offered on classic Porsche models (notably on early 911s in the 1980s). It was used on Porsche models like the early 911 (e.g., 1982–1984 911 SC and Carrera). So if you see “Zinc Metallic” on a Porsche’s build sheet or used-car listing, it’s referring to this classic metallic silver shade.

name: Titan Metallic
notes: One of a large number of Porsche variations on the smokey grey theme. It's a pure metallic grey with no blue overtones. It's named for the race of Ancient Greek gods, and one of Saturn's moons.

name: Titanium
notes: Titanium, like Meteor Grey and Seal Grey and other metallic greys in this general family have been popular Porsche shades since the late 1980s.

name: Tobacco Metallic
notes: Tobacco is another variation on the metallic bronze theme. It's more gold than say Copper Brown Metallic. It's similar to an equally rare Lotus color Tawny Gold, and the Ferrari color called Nocciola (Hazelnut). Get past your brown car phobia people, this is a striking color, particularly on an early Turbo or a 928.

name: Togo Brown
notes: Togo is a straightforward shade of medium non-metallic brown. Togo was seldom ordered in period, and over the years, a fair number of Togo Brown cars have been resprayed in other more common colors like red, white or silver, making it even more unusual to see the color today.

name: Topaz Brown Metallic
notes: A light grey-brown metallic that is a perfect, understated fit for the first-generation Panamera.

name: Tourmaline (Turmalin) Green Metallic
notes: A striking, jewel-toned shade, Tourmaline Green Metallic blends deep green with pronounced blue undertones, echoing the color of the gemstone it’s named for. In bright light, it reveals a vivid teal shimmer; in lower light, it settles into a darker, more mysterious green-blue. Rare, memorable, and unmistakably early-’90s Porsche, it’s one of those colors that feels both adventurous and refined - a true standout in the lineup.

name: Truffle Brown Metallic
notes: A new color for the Porsche Panamera, truffle brown metallic comes in with a light but creamy dark brown tone. It glistens in the sun and is sleek at night.

name: Turkey Green
notes: A bold, vintage-leaning shade, Turkey Green sits in the lighter, yellow-green part of the spectrum — bright, lively, and unmistakably period-correct. It carries a playful, almost retro character, with a sunny green tone that feels more spirited than traditional dark Porsche greens. Rarely seen today, Turkey Green offers a refreshing pop of color that instantly stands out while still fitting comfortably within Porsche’s classic palette.

name: Turkish Red
notes: Turkish Red is a darker purer red than Strawberry or Signal Red.

name: Turquoise
notes: True to its name, Turquoise takes inspiration from the semi-precious stone. This rare Porsche color is often mistaken for Gulf Blue but is notably greener and darker. Its unique tone gives it a distinctive charm—one that, despite its beauty, was chosen by only a few buyers.

name: Turquoise Blue
notes: Tropical freshness: the shade Turquoiseblue has a special aura that is reminiscent of the waters of a tropical island. The attractive shade of Turquoiseblue conveys a feeling of fresh energy and impresses with its light and airy sophistication. The color was available in Porsche's colour program for a few years at the end of the 1990s and is now available as a Paint to Sample option. Turquoiseblue adds a refreshing accent and conveys the relaxing feeling of looking out at the sea. Visually, Turquoiseblue is similar to the colours Rivierablue and Mexicoblue, although it is a little darker and has subtle green undertones.

name: Turquoise Green
notes: A vibrant blend of green and blue, Turquoise Green sits right at the crossroads between the two hues. It’s brighter and more playful than Porsche’s traditional greens, with a clean, aquatic tone that shifts subtly depending on the light. Sunlight brings out its crisp, almost tropical character, while shade reveals a richer green depth. Eye-catching but not over the top, it’s a color that brings an unmistakably fresh energy to any Porsche.

name: Turquoise Metallic
notes: A vivid fusion of blue and green, Turquoise Metallic stands out with its bright, high-energy presence. The metallic flake adds dimension, giving the color a shimmering, almost aquatic quality that shifts with movement and light. In full sun it leans toward a bold, tropical blue-green; in softer light it reveals a deeper, cooler tone. Uncommon and unmistakable, it’s a color that brings a lively, expressive personality to any Porsche.

name: Ultra Blue
notes: Ultra Blue is a lesser-known Porsche shade often mistaken for Albert Blue or Aga Blue. Sitting somewhere between the two in tone, it’s a beautifully balanced medium blue—subtle, elegant, and quietly distinctive despite its understated character.

name: Ultraviolet
notes: Unconventional: Ultraviolet is a non-metallic, medium violet and could be perceived as a modern interpretation of the color Lilac from the 1970s. The term 'Violet' is derived from the French word 'viola' for the flower of the same name. And of course 'ultra' means to go beyond. Ultraviolet has a strong character that embodies a sense of daring, freedom and originality. The color creates a unique and striking visual effect that can evoke strong emotions. Ultraviolet attracts attention but also stimulates the imagination. For the market launch of the 911 GT3 RS in 2015, Ultraviolet was chosen as the communication color.

name: Umbra
notes: Porsche's Umbra Metallic is a dark, sophisticated metallic color. It is described as looking "beastly" and powerful, appearing bright and clean while showcasing the power of the vehicle. The color is a metallic base coat, often appearing as a dark, rich shade, that is applied with a finish that allows the metallic effect to stand out. Classified as uncommon to rare by some sources, though the rarity can vary depending on the model and year.

name: Umbra Metallic
notes: Umbra Metallic looks beastly, and does not fail to show off the power in which the machine holds. It shines bright and clean and was made to handle anything.

name: Underberg Green
notes: Forest foliage: Underberggreen is a modern and exciting shade of green that represents both the company color and the name of the Swiss entrepreneurial family. The bold and striking green color originates from the company logo and the paintwork used for the delivery vans of the digestif bitters producer Underberg. Underberggreen was developed at the personal request of a customer as part of the Paint to Sample Plus process and is now included in the Paint to Sample offering. The shade of Underberggreen can vary slightly depending on the lighting, but it is a dark and rich green that resembles the color of evergreen trees or dark forest foliage. As a deep and vibrant shade of green, the color conveys a feeling of sophistication, quality and tradition.

name: Urban Bamboo ChromaFlair
notes: Porsche Urban Bamboo ChromaFlair, like all ChromaFlair paints shifts colors depending on the lighting and viewing angle. For those who are curious about how the magic happens, tiny flakes of aluminum coated with shiny magnesium fluoride give a prism-like effect to the finish.

name: Vanadium Grey Metallic
notes: Vanadium Grey Metallic was first introduced by Porsche on the 718 Spyder RS in 2023. It is now available on the 911, 718 Boxster, 718 Cayman and 718 Spyder RS. The name draws from the chemical element vanadium (element number 23) - a lustrous, silvery-grey metal that reflects the paint's sleek, metallic appearance. The shade is a darker grey with vibrant, silver metallic flake that gives it depth, technical flair, and a sporty edge-perfectly aligning with Porsche's high-performance identity. Vanadium Grey Metallic replaced Agate Grey metallic as the go-to grey metallic option on 992.2-series 911's. In the 718 configurator Vanadium Grey Metallic is a Metallic Option Color while in the 911 range it is a Shades Option Paint.

name: Velvet Purple Metallic
notes: Royal appearance: Velvet Purple Metallic is a rich, deep purple shade reminiscent of the luxurious fabric it is named after. The shade exudes a soft, velvety surface appearance that gives the impression of a plush, almost tactile quality. This creates a warm and elegant look with a luxurious and royal class. The color was originally used by Audi under the color code LQ87. The color is chosen here for high-engine models such as the Audi RS Q8. Velvet Purple Metallic is a dark, stylish color with a slight red undertone. The combination of the clear, rich velvet purple with the metallic finish creates a rich and sumptuous aura.

name: Velvet Red Metallic
notes: It's a pity Velvet Red didn't stick around too long, it really is quite attractive. Porsche color names usually fall into one of two categories--terribly abstract or literally descriptive. This one falls into the latter category. Imagine a yard of red velvet fabric, or Liberace's curtains, and that describes this color to a T.

name: Venetian Blue Metallic
notes: Venetian Blue is an understated metallic shade that blends medium blue with subtle gray tones. Elegant and reserved, it carries a refined metallic depth that complements Porsche’s design language with timeless sophistication.

name: Venice Blue Metallic
notes: Venice Blue is a pleasing grayish-blue with a subtle touch of green, similar in character to the earlier Petrol Blue. The name pays tribute to the glassmaking tradition of Venice, Italy, reflecting both craftsmanship and timeless elegance.

name: Verde British Racing Green
notes: Verde British Racing Green is a rich and deep dark green color that is often associated with speed, performance and luxury. The shade was developed due to a specific customer request as part of the Paint to Sample Plus program and was later incorporated into the Paint to Sample program. Verde British Racing Green has bluish-dark undertones with a hint of yellow, giving the color depth and vibrancy when exposed to sunlight. Overall, Verde British Racing Green is a sophisticated and timeless color that exudes elegance and prestige.

name: Vesuvio Metallic
notes: Porsche Vesuvio Metallic is one of the more curious colors to be offered by Porsche. It's appearance is highly dependent on the lighting. In shadows, on a cloudy day or in other weak light situations, it's quite grey. In direct sunlight, there is a very strong purple cast to it. Vesuvio is one of a handful of colors that bridged the air-cooled to water-cooled era. It was available on both the 993 and the 996. It's named for the famous volcano that overlooks the city of Naples.

name: Vesuvius Grey Metallic
notes: Porsche's Vesuvius Grey Metallic (also known as Vesuvio Metallic) is a chameleon-like color that appears as a medium to dark grey in low light but reveals a distinct purple hue in direct sunlight, inspired by the Italian volcano of the same name. This unique appearance, combined with its metallic fleck, makes it an elegant and versatile shade that has transitioned from air-cooled to water-cooled and modern Porsche models, often reappearing through the 2023 Paint-to-Sample (PTS) program. The paint's appearance dramatically changes depending on the lighting. In the shade, it looks like a traditional grey, while direct sunlight brings out a prominent purple undertone. Due to its complex and shifting nature, the color is often described as understated and elegant, suiting a wide range of high-performance and luxury vehicles. Vesuvius Metallic was originally offered on models from the air-cooled to water-cooled eras, such as the 993 and 996 generations of the 911.

name: Viola Metallic
notes: Dramatic beauty: Viola Metallic is a bold, dark purple with a captivating metallic sheen. In Latin, 'Viola' means violet, a genus of plants that produces dark and rich purple petals. Viola Metallic is a historic Porsche color that was first offered in 1993. The color was available for the 911 models of the 964, 993 and 996 generations under the color codes 3AE and 39G as well as under the name of 'Viola Pearl'. At the end of the 1990s it became available on the Boxster (986). Viola Metallic is a medium to dark shade that can also be described as classy and elegant. The metallic effect creates a dynamic paint surface, creating a unique jewel-like appearance. In low light conditions the color looks almost black.

name: Viola Purple Metallic
notes: Pristine and creative: Viola Purple Metallic is a rich shade of purple whose name and shade were inspired by the violet flower, which has purple petals. The color is based on the historic Porsche color 'Viola Blue Metallic', which was offered under the color codes 37E and 3AF in 1993 for 928, 964 and 968. Viola Purple Metallic is not to be confused with Viola Metallic, which is a much darker color. Viola Purple Metallic is a deep, cool shade of purple that can range from dark and moody to bright and bold, depending on its intensity. The shade has characteristic blue undertones, which give Viola Purple Metallic a slightly cooler and more muted appearance than other shades of purple. The color is often associated with royalty, luxury and sophistication. What's special is that Viola Purple Metallic can change in different lighting conditions to the extent that the shade can almost look like a completely different color.

name: Violet Blue Metallic
notes: In a sure sign that the conservative 80s were over, Porsche started experimenting with some wild jewel-tones in the 1990s and Violet Blue Metallic was one of the most extroverted of these colors. It's a gemstone-like purple with bluish overtones, and it was particularly scarce on the 968 cabriolet. Just one is accounted for in North America.

name: Violet Chromaflair
notes: Violet Chromaflair is a color that includes a special pigment that shifts colors depending on the light and viewing angle--it's grey, no, it's purple, maybe greenish, depending on the light. It was the color specified for the 1999 Millennium Edition 996.

name: Viper Green
notes: Trendsetter: almost no other color, apart from white, is as closely associated with the 911 Carrera RS 2.7 as Vipergreen. The extroverted color is a bright and strong shade of green, reminiscent of the color of a viper snake. Almost neon, Vipergreen creates a bold impression with its saturated look and highlights the high-performance features of RS models. Vipergreen was available from 1972-1973 and again in the 2010s. It is available as a Paint to Sample option for the Porsche models 911, 718 and Taycan as well as the Panamera and Macan. Vipergreen is a specific shade that creates an unmistakable radiance with its bright appearance and slight yellow undertone. The striking green vehicle color is often used for accent parts to create contrast and thereby draw attention to components such as the rear wing or the air intakes.

name: Viper Green Metallic
notes: Viper Green Metallic - not to be confused with the better-known non-metallic 911 color of the same name - is a vivid, metallic lime green that blends classic Porsche boldness with a modern, almost Mamba Green–like energy. Its fine metallic flake adds depth and shimmer, giving the color a lively, high-impact presence in sunlight and a slightly richer tone in the shade. Like most 914 colors, it originated from the VW palette rather than Porsche’s own, and it was offered for just a single year. Rare, eye-catching, and perfectly suited to the 914’s lines, it remains one of the most distinctive greens of the era.

name: Volcano Grey Metallic
notes: Volcano Grey is another in a seemingly endless number of variations on the dark metallic grey theme. Porsche AG and Porsche buyers are positively addicted to colors like this.

name: Voodoo Blue
notes: Under a spell: Voodooblue is a strong, intense shade of blue and a modern interpretation of the classic Porsche colours Mexicoblue and Arrowblue. The colour is a bright, electrifying shade of blue that is intense in its radiance. The name 'voodoo' refers to the mystical and enchanting quality of the colour, which exudes a sense of energy and adventure. Voodooblue is a rich blue with hints of green that resembles the colors of the Caribbean Sea. Known for its bold and vibrant appearance, the shade is a popular choice for sporty and adventurous vehicles such as off-road vehicles and rally cars. It is a bold color that attracts attention and stands out in traffic.

name: White
notes: The purest expression of form — Porsche’s White is a clean, neutral hue that strips everything down to design and detail. It’s neither warm nor cool, sitting dead-center: bright, direct, and honest. There’s no metallic, no pearl, no tint — just uncompromising clarity. In sunlight, it’s brilliant and high-contrast, amplifying every edge, crease, and curve of the bodywork. In softer light, it smooths out to a calm, architectural white that gives the car a disciplined, almost minimalist presence. It’s the color that refuses to hide anything — every panel gap, every reflection, every shadow stands out with purpose. White is the purist’s choice — timeless, clean, and precise. It’s not about nostalgia or trend; it’s about letting the engineering speak for itself. A Porsche in white doesn’t need attention — it demands respect..

name: White Gold Metallic
notes: As the name implies, White Gold Metallic is a warm silver with a touch of yellow gold in it.

name: Willow Green
notes: Soothing and gentle: Willowgreen is a soft and calming shade of green named after the willow tree. The tree is known for its long, slender leaves and branches that sway gently in the wind – as well as its foliage, which develops a brownish-green to yellow color. It is a historic Porsche color that was available on the 914 in the early 1970s under the color code 4310 / 223 / L63K. Willowgreen is a strong yet understated color with subtle grey or brown undertones that give it a slightly earthy note. Willowgreen can vary in intensity from a medium pastel green to a darker, richer shade. Overall, Willowgreen radiates peace and harmony.

name: Wimbledon Green Metallic
notes: Wimbledon Green Metallic is essentially a metallic turquoise-green — a vivid, slightly blue-leaning shade that stands out far more than its name might suggest. It’s a scarce color, and it looks especially striking when paired with the matching green interiors once available on models like the 928. Despite its British-sounding name, it refers to the famous All England Lawn Tennis & Croquet Club, with which Porsche has had longstanding sponsorship ties. The color carries that same energetic, summery personality: bright, playful, and unmistakably unique within the Porsche palette.

name: Wine Red Metallic
notes: Another literally descriptive Porsche color name (if you're into Rose that comes out of a box), it's easily confused with the earlier predominantly 924 color Indiana Red. It's quite an attractive shade, which makes one wonder why it's so scarce.

name: Yachting Blue Metallic
notes: Refreshing breeze: Yachtingbluemetallic is a subtle metallic blue inspired by the many different shades of blue present in the world of yachts and sailing. The paint is often used for boat hulls and sail covers. The color was introduced in 2010 as one of the ten standard colors for the first generation of the Porsche Panamera (Type 970). Yachtingbluemetallic is a rich, deep shade of blue that can vary from a slightly greenish blue to a darker navy blue. The metallic shimmer gives the color a classic elegance. Yachtingbluemetallic has slightly cool undertones that impart a refreshing feeling. The color is often described as a mix of navy and royal blues with a touch of grey, adding a special emphasis to the body shape of a car.

name: Zambezi (Forest) Green
notes: Zambezi Green was one of several greens offered on the 914, and—like many 914 colors—it was shared with VW models of the era. Named after the iconic African river, it’s a rich, medium-to-dark green with a natural, almost forest-like character. In 1975 the name was changed to Forest Green, though the color code and the shade itself remained unchanged. Though originally a 914 color, it has occasionally resurfaced through Porsche’s Paint-to-Sample program, where it continues to look right at home on modern cars. The 996 shown in the image library is a great example of how timeless and versatile this shade can be.

name: Zanzibar Red
notes: Zanzibar Red is actually a dark metallic orange with a hint of copper in it. It's inexplicably rare for such a stunning color. Zanzibar is world-renowned as the "Spice Island." The color mirrors the rich, earthy palettes of the spices exported from the region—specifically cloves, nutmeg, and cinnamon. These spices possess that same warm, reddish-orange glow found in the paint.

name: Zenith Blue Metallic
notes: Zenith Blue is one of Porsche’s most striking metallic blues, known for the strong purple cast it can take on in certain light. It’s also notable for spanning both the air-cooled and water-cooled eras: offered on the 993 and later the 996, as well as the Boxster, making it one of the few colors to bridge that transition.

name: Zermatt Silver
notes: Not the most common silver but nonetheless so beautiful. Zermatt Silver has a darker side to it and almost looks like a gold/brown tint in the sun.

name: Zermatt Silver Metallic
notes: Zermatt Silver is a warm shade of silver named for the Swiss ski resort town.

name: Zyclam Red Pearl Effect
notes: Zyclam Red Pearl was an extremely rare late 944 color that looks extraordinary attractive on the 944 S2 Cabriolet. Sadly, white, black, silver and Guards Red cars outnumber Zyclam Red cars by about 2500 to 1. Zycmamen is the german word for Cyclamen, which is a type of european flower. The color of the flower almost exactly matches the paint color. Zyclamrot is probably what we would call Cyclamen Red.
